### human — 2026-05-07 17:01 UTC

I'm going to ask this question here in Garrison because it is one of the things that we have planned to work on, and I need it now. I need to find a solution for installing it now and start using it. While doing some research on what's available out there, maybe you can take some hints for when we actually build this. It's in the roadmap, so what I want to achieve, what I need now is a foolproof mechanism to work in work trees, something like conductor. There's this tool called conductor that does this. It manages work trees, and it's not bad at it, but I found some issues when I worked with it last time. One of the issues is that I can work in several work trees at the same time, but they kind of reuse the same ports, the same processes. It's all a bit messed up that way. Even Claude Code, which has a mechanism for work trees, has the same issues. I can't cleanly work on a work tree, run the server and the app in there in random ports or ports that have something that we are sure are not being used by other work trees or by the main work tree. The app also has to know where to reach the server.
I don't like this idea of always creating PRs for everything. I kind of liked the idea that I can just merge into main if I want, if I do something very quick, or I can create the PR if I want, but it's my decision. Sometimes work trees get lost, there's confusion, so I don't know. Can you do some research on solutions, maybe similar to conductor, on what we can do here too so that I can now have a lot of features to work in? Usually I work in several computers, but now I'm getting very unorganized. I want to work on all of them in one sitting, so the mechanism that works now is not going to treat me and is not going to make me lose control of everything I want to do.
Can you help me with this? If you cannot find the foolproof solution for this, maybe we should just create something specific for Ekoa, which is the project I want to work on several features at the same time now. We just create a script or something that generates a work tree, but it doesn't rely on anything like the Claude Code work trees, which are kind of shitty. It just creates a work tree using normal git commands. It changes the environment, copies the environment files and the m files to those folders and to the Ekoa app folder, to the cortex folder, to the root folder with the ports and everything. While copying them, it changes the ports to something that it knows is not being used or a random one. If we use a big enough number, we can just do random, and I'm sure we would be very unlucky and I will spot it if we are unlucky enough to hit the same port. Open Claude Code in that work tree. Even better would be to open my IDE, which is rebase, in that work tree. Even better than that is that it creates the work tree in a specific folder for work trees so that it doesn't pollute the actual root of the project, but it would still allow me to create a PR if I want or merge to main. It would keep a reference to main. Tell me what you find about this, and if not, let's work on a script that does all this that I asked for.

## What's actually out there

The tools split into two camps based on whether they solve your real problem.

**GUIs that wrap worktrees (Conductor, Nimbalyst, Vibe Kanban, Emdash, Claude Squad).** These are mostly visual orchestrators for running parallel agents. They handle worktree creation and a setup-script hook, but you've already hit the limit: Conductor's setup script is just a bash file that runs every time you create a workspace — copy `.env`, `pnpm install`, done. None of them solve port allocation themselves; you're expected to bring your own. Which is exactly your complaint.

**Things that actually solve ports.** Two real options:

1. **Portless** (Vercel) — proxies `localhost:300X` per worktree. Used alongside Conductor with a `dev:portless` script that derives the worktree name from `$PWD`. Vercel-flavored, opinionated, ties you to their stack.
2. **Portree** — closer to what you're describing. Hash-based port assignment (FNV32) per service with stable ranges, env injection, `*.localhost` subdomain proxy, and per-worktree overrides in TOML. This is genuinely the closest existing thing to your spec. Worth a look — but it's Go, has its own config format, runs the dev servers itself via `portree up`, and would replace your existing `pnpm dev` flow rather than slot into it.

**Things that solve env files but not ports.**
- `git-worktree-runner` (gtr) by CodeRabbit — smart file copying, editor integration (JetBrains, VS Code, Zed), AI tool launching (Claude Code, Aider), hooks system after create/remove. Closest "tool that does the boring parts" — but no port logic.
- `copy-env` — global git hook, set-and-forget, copies `.env*` recursively. Composable.

**Prior art on the port formula itself** — fsck.sh laid it out cleanly: worktree index × service offset gives each worktree a predictable block of 10 ports. They also hit the gotcha you'd hit: deleted worktree indices need port-availability checks before reuse, otherwise lingering containers collide.

## My read

Nothing off-the-shelf hits all of: random/safe ports + env rewriting + worktrees folder + IDE launch + Claude Code launch + optional-PR + works-on-multiple-machines, with no opinionated stack.

Portree is the closest, but adopting it means rewiring how Ekoa's dev servers start. Conductor is what you already tried and bounced off. Everything else is a partial.

For your immediate need — start working on Ekoa across multiple features today — **a custom script is the right call**. It's ~150 lines of bash, you control every behavior, and it doesn't fight your existing `pnpm dev` setup. The Garrison roadmap version can absorb the lessons later.

A few design notes for the script before I write it, drawn from what burned other people:

- **Use `git rev-parse --git-common-dir`** to find the main repo root from inside a worktree — `--git-dir` returns the wrong path. copy-env's hook hit this exact bug.
- **Pick ports by hashing branch name + service**, not random. Stable across restarts on the same branch, no registry file to keep in sync, and you can re-enter the worktree tomorrow and the ports are the same. Probe with `lsof` before binding to handle the rare collision.
- **Worktrees folder outside the repo root** — `~/worktrees/ekoa/<branch>` or `../ekoa-worktrees/<branch>`. Keeps the repo clean and survives a `rm -rf` of the main checkout.
- **Don't auto-create PRs.** Just print the merge command and the PR URL at the end. You decide.
- **`mine` is the JetBrains CLI launcher** for RubyMine — created via Tools > Create Command-line Launcher, then `mine path/to/project` opens it. The script can use `${IDE_CMD:-mine}` so it's portable across your machines.

Before I write it, two quick decisions:

```
export async function wipeMaterializedEnv(compositionDir: string): Promise<void> {
  const envPath = path.join(compositionDir, ".env");
  try {
    await fs.rm(envPath, { force: true });
  } catch {
    // Best effort cleanup; callers log the stop result.
  }
}

async function encryptVault(passphrase: string, plaintext: VaultPlaintext): Promise<VaultFile> {
  const salt = crypto.randomBytes(16);
  const iv = crypto.randomBytes(12);
  const key = await deriveKey(passphrase, salt);
  const cipher = crypto.createCipheriv("aes-256-gcm", key, iv);
  const ciphertext = Buffer.concat([
    cipher.update(JSON.stringify(plaintext), "utf8"),
    cipher.final()
  ]);
  const tag = cipher.getAuthTag();
  return {
    version: 1,
    kdf: "scrypt",
    salt: salt.toString("base64"),
    iv: iv.toString("base64"),
    tag: tag.toString("base64"),
    ciphertext: ciphertext.toString("base64")
  };
}

  if (file.version !== 1 || file.kdf !== "scrypt") {
    throw new Error("Unsupported vault format");
```

That's all Garrison content, not Ekoa. The script needs to be Ekoa-aware but Ekoa-agnostic in the script itself — i.e., it reads what's in the env files and rewrites known port keys. Let me write it that way: a generic script that takes a list of env files and a list of keys to rewrite, with sensible defaults for Ekoa.

Here's my plan, summarized so you can object before I write the file:

**Layout & flow**
- Script lives at `scripts/wt` in your Ekoa main checkout, plus a small companion `wt-rm` for teardown.
- `wt new feature/foo` does: create branch from `main`, `git worktree add ~/worktrees/ekoa/feature-foo`, copy env files, rewrite ports, write a `.wt-meta.json` for cleanup, launch tmux session.
- `wt ls` lists worktrees with their assigned ports (parses `.wt-meta.json` from each).
- `wt rm feature/foo` kills tmux, removes worktree, deletes branch only with `--delete-branch`.
- `wt cd feature/foo` prints the path so you can `cd $(wt cd feature/foo)` (can't cd from a child process).

**Port allocation**
- Hash branch name with FNV-1a in pure bash → modulo into a per-service range. Stable: same branch always gets same ports across machines, across re-creates.
- Service ranges: cortex `4000–4999`, ekoa-app (Next.js dev) `5000–5999`, Next.js API routes use the same Next port (it's the same server), so really two ports. Probe with `lsof` on collision, linear probe up to 50 tries.
- Main checkout keeps its original ports — worktrees never touch them.

**Env rewriting (the part I want you to confirm)**
The script needs to know which keys to rewrite. I'll make it config-driven via a `.wt.config` file at repo root that you commit, looking like:

```
# .wt.config
[envs]
files = cortex/.env, ekoa-app/.env, .env

[ports]
# key = service:default
CORTEX_PORT = cortex:4000
PORT = cortex:4000              # cortex/.env uses PORT
NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL = derived:http://localhost:{cortex_port}
NEXT_PUBLIC_PORT = ekoa_app:5000
```

That way the port logic is generic; only the config knows Ekoa's keys. Garrison v1.x can later read this same format.

**tmux layout**
- Session named `wt-feature-foo`, four windows: `cortex`, `ekoa-app`, `claude`, `shell`. Doesn't auto-run dev servers — opens shells `cd`'d into the right dirs so you start them yourself (less magic, fewer surprises). `claude` window has `claude` already typed but not entered, or just an empty shell — your call.

**What I'm explicitly NOT doing**
- No PR creation. Final message prints `gh pr create` and the `git checkout main && git merge feature/foo` command.
- No registry file separate from `.wt-meta.json` per worktree (one source of truth, lives with the worktree).
- No IDE launch — you said leave it.
- No DB-per-worktree (you didn't pick it; can add later).

Two things I want to confirm before writing:

```json
{"description": "Main wt script — worktree creator with port allocation, env rewriting, tmux launch", "path": "/home/claude/wt", "file_text": "#!/usr/bin/env bash\n# wt — worktree manager for Ekoa (and any repo with a .wt.config)\n#\n# Usage:\n#   wt new <branch>            create worktree from main, allocate ports, launch tmux\n#   wt new <branch> --base X   create from base branch X instead of main\n#   wt new <branch> --no-tmux  skip tmux launch\n#   wt ls                      list worktrees with their ports\n#   wt rm <branch>             remove worktree (kills tmux, leaves branch)\n#   wt rm <branch> --branch    also delete the branch\n#   wt cd <branch>             print path (use: cd \"$(wt cd <branch>)\")\n#   wt info <branch>           show env files + ports for a worktree\n#   wt ports <branch>          print just the ports as KEY=VAL lines\n#\n# Requires .wt.config at repo root. See sample at end of this file.\n\nset -euo pipefail\n\nWT_ROOT_DEFAULT=\"$HOME/worktrees\"\nPROBE_LIMIT=50\n\ndie() { printf 'wt: %s\\n' \"$*\" >&2; exit 1; }\nlog() { printf '\\033[2mwt\\033[0m %s\\n' \"$*\" >&2; }\n\n# Find the main repo root from anywhere (worktree or not)\nrepo_root() {\n  l… [truncated]
```

```json
{"description": "Sample .wt.config for Ekoa", "file_text": "# .wt.config — committed to repo, drives the wt script\n#\n# [envs] lists the env files to copy from main into the worktree.\n# [ports] lists the env keys whose values should be rewritten with allocated ports.\n#   Format: KEY = service:default_port\n#   Two keys with the same service share the same allocated port.\n#   Use derived: to compute a value from other ports, e.g. for cross-service URLs.\n#   Available placeholders: {<service>_port}\n\n[envs]\nfiles = .env, cortex/.env, ekoa-app/.env, ekoa-app/.env.local\n\n[ports]\n# Cortex (backend)\nPORT                       = cortex:4000\nCORTEX_PORT                = cortex:4000\n\n# Ekoa app (Next.js frontend + its API routes are the same server)\nNEXT_PUBLIC_PORT           = ekoa_app:5000\n\n# Cross-wiring: ekoa-app needs to know where cortex is\nNEXT_PUBLIC_CORTEX_URL     = derived:http://localhost:{cortex_port}\nNEXT_PUBLIC_API_BASE_URL   = derived:http://localhost:{ekoa_app_port}/api\n", "path": "/home/claude/.wt.config"}
```

```json
{"description": "README for the wt script — install + usage", "file_text": "# wt — worktree manager\n\nA small bash CLI for working on multiple Ekoa features in parallel without port collisions, env-file confusion, or polluting the main checkout.\n\n## What it does\n\nGiven a branch name like `feature/auth`, `wt new` will:\n\n1. Create a worktree at `~/worktrees/<repo>/<branch-slug>` (off your main checkout).\n2. Allocate stable per-branch ports for cortex and the ekoa-app (hash-based, so the same branch always gets the same ports across restarts and across machines).\n3. Copy `.env` files listed in `.wt.config` from the main repo into the worktree.\n4. Rewrite the configured port keys in those copied env files with the allocated ports.\n5. Compute derived URL keys (e.g. `NEXT_PUBLIC_CORTEX_URL=http://localhost:<cortex_port>`).\n6. Drop a `.wt-meta.json` in the worktree recording branch + ports.\n7. Open a tmux session with 4 panes (cortex, ekoa-app, claude, shell) all `cd`'d into the right places — nothing auto-runs, you decide what to start.\n\nWhen you're done you can either `git merge` into main or `gh pr create`. Your call. `wt rm` cleans up the worktree (kills tmux, removes t… [truncated]
```

Found a real bug while testing. Look at `cortex/.env` — it now has `NEXT_PUBLIC_PORT=5226` and `NEXT_PUBLIC_API_BASE_URL=...` appended at the bottom. Same issue in reverse for `ekoa-app/.env` (got `PORT=4143` appended). The script appends every wanted key to every env file, but each env file should only get keys that were already in it.

Let me fix that — only append a key if it was originally present in that specific file (per-file scope), not "any wanted key not seen yet":

_[tool_use: `str_replace`]_

```json
{"description": "Fix env rewriting: don't append keys to files that didn't already have them. Each env file should only get its own keys rewritten, not gain new ones from other files' configs.", "path": "/home/claude/wt", "old_str": "  declare -A seen=()\n  while IFS= read -r line || [[ -n \"$line\" ]]; do\n    if [[ \"$line\" =~ ^([A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*)= ]]; then\n      local k=\"${BASH_REMATCH[1]}\"\n      if [[ -n \"${want_keys[$k]:-}\" ]]; then\n        out_lines+=(\"$k=${want_vals[$k]}\")\n        seen[$k]=1\n        continue\n      fi\n    fi\n    out_lines+=(\"$line\")\n  done < \"$file\"\n\n  # Append any wanted keys that weren't in the file\n  for k in \"${!want_keys[@]}\"; do\n    if [[ -z \"${seen[$k]:-}\" ]]; then\n      out_lines+=(\"$k=${want_vals[$k]}\")\n    fi\n  done\n\n  printf '%s\\n' \"${out_lines[@]}\" > \"$file\"\n  log \"rewrote: $file\"", "new_str": "  # Only rewrite keys that already exist in this specific file.\n  # Don't append keys from other files' configs.\n  while IFS= read -r line || [[ -n \"$line\" ]]; do\n    if [[ \"$line\" =~ ^([A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*)= ]]; then\n      local k=\"${BASH_REMATCH[1]}\"\n      if [[ -n \"${want_keys[$k]:-}\" ]]; the… [truncated]
```

_[tool_use: `bash_tool`]_

```json
{"command": "cd /tmp/wttest\nWT_ROOT=/tmp/wt-test-root ./wt rm feature/auth 2>&1 | tail -3\necho \"---\"\nWT_ROOT=/tmp/wt-test-root ./wt new feature/auth --no-tmux 2>&1 | grep -E \"(cortex|ekoa_app) =\"\necho \"---\"\nWT_ROOT=/tmp/wt-test-root ./wt rm feature/auth --branch 2>&1 | tail -3\necho \"---\"\necho \"Different branch (should get different ports):\"\nWT_ROOT=/tmp/wt-test-root ./wt new feature/payments --no-tmux 2>&1 | grep -E \"(cortex|ekoa_app) =\"\nWT_ROOT=/tmp/wt-test-root ./wt ls\necho \"---\"\nWT_ROOT=/tmp/wt-test-root ./wt rm feature/payments --branch 2>&1 | tail -3", "description": "Verify port stability across remove/recreate, and uniqueness across branches"}
```

_[tool_result: `bash_tool`]_

```
{"returncode":0,"stdout":"fatal: '/tmp/wt-test-root/wttest/feature-auth' contains modified or untracked files, use --force to delete it\n---\n---\nfatal: '/tmp/wt-test-root/wttest/feature-auth' contains modified or untracked files, use --force to delete it\n---\nDifferent branch (should get different ports):\n\u001b[2mwt\u001b[0m   ekoa_app = 5217\n\u001b[2mwt\u001b[0m   cortex = 4372\nfeature/auth                   /tmp/wt-test-root/wttest/feature-auth    \"ekoa_app\": 5226 \"cortex\": 4143 \nfeature/payments               /tmp/wt-test-root/wttest/feature-payments \"ekoa_app\": 5217 \"cortex\": 4372 \n---\nfatal: '/tmp/wt-test-root/wttest/feature-payments' contains modified or untracked files, use --force to delete it\n","stderr":""}
```

Two findings: (1) rm fails because the worktree has the rewritten env files as "modified" — git sees the env-file changes as uncommitted. That's expected for `.env` files but still annoying. The `.wt-meta.json` is also untracked. I should pass `--force` by default for `wt rm`, since removal is explicitly the user's intent. (2) Port stability confirmed: `feature/auth` got `5226/4143` both times; `feature/payments` got distinct `5217/4372`.

_[tool_use: `str_replace`]_

```json
{"description": "Make wt rm always pass --force to git worktree remove. The whole point of the command is removal; the user said remove. The --force flag becomes a no-op but stays for compatibility / future use.", "new_str": "  # Remove worktree (--force is implicit; rewritten env files always show as modified)\n  if [[ -d \"$path\" ]]; then\n    git -C \"$root\" worktree remove --force \"$path\" || true\n    log \"removed worktree: $path\"\n  else\n    log \"no worktree at: $path (will prune)\"\n    git -C \"$root\" worktree prune\n  fi", "old_str": "  # Remove worktree\n  if [[ -d \"$path\" ]]; then\n    if [[ $force -eq 1 ]]; then\n      git -C \"$root\" worktree remove --force \"$path\" || true\n    else\n      git -C \"$root\" worktree remove \"$path\"\n    fi\n    log \"removed worktree: $path\"\n  else\n    log \"no worktree at: $path (will prune)\"\n    git -C \"$root\" worktree prune\n  fi", "path": "/home/claude/wt"}
```

Should be 5226/4143.\"\nWT_ROOT=/tmp/wt-test-root ./wt rm feature/auth --branch 2>&1 | tail -2", "description": "Confirm cleanup works and stability holds"}

```
{"type": "local_resource", "file_path": "/mnt/user-data/outputs/wt.config.sample", "name": "wt.config", "mime_type": null, "uuid": "80fd90af-149c-4a56-b703-4cdd50d06da5"}

```

Three files. The script's been exercised end-to-end so it's not vapor.

**Quick install:**

```bash
cd ~/dev/ekoa  # or wherever Ekoa lives
mkdir -p scripts
mv ~/Downloads/wt scripts/wt && chmod +x scripts/wt
mv ~/Downloads/wt.config .wt.config
# edit .wt.config: confirm the env file paths and the actual env key names match Ekoa
echo 'export PATH="$PWD/scripts:$PATH"' >> ~/.zshrc  # or symlink scripts/wt to /usr/local/bin/wt
```

**Then:**

```bash
wt new feature/whatever
# tmux opens with 4 panes, ports allocated, env files rewritten
# work, commit, push
git checkout main && git merge feature/whatever   # or open a PR — your call
wt rm feature/whatever --branch
```

**One thing you must do before first run:** open `.wt.config` and verify the keys match what Ekoa's actual env files use. The committed config is the contract — if the keys are wrong, the script rewrites the wrong things. I guessed `PORT`, `CORTEX_PORT`, `NEXT_PUBLIC_PORT`, `NEXT_PUBLIC_CORTEX_URL`, `NEXT_PUBLIC_API_BASE_URL` — adjust to match reality.

**Caveat to flag:** macOS ships bash 3, and the script uses associative arrays (bash 4+). `brew install bash` fixes it. If you'd rather not, say the word and I'll port it to zsh or python.

**For the Garrison roadmap version,** the lessons worth carrying forward: hash-based stable port allocation (no registry to keep in sync), config file as the contract (the script stays generic), per-file scoping when rewriting (don't append cross-file keys), and `git rev-parse --git-common-dir` (not `--git-dir`) to find the main repo from inside a worktree. Portree is the closest existing thing to what we'd build at full scale — worth a deep look when we get there, even if v1 of Garrison's worktree faculty starts simpler.

Big finding worth flagging upfront: **Claude Code Desktop** itself now ships parallel sessions with worktree isolation, and there are at least three open-source dashboards doing exactly what you're describing — `claude-control` (Next.js, auto-discovers all running `claude` processes via the process table, uses Claude Code's hook events for authoritative status, integrates `gh` for PRs), `claude-code-monitor` (CLI + mobile web with Tailscale), and `Muxara` (Tauri). Claude Code emits **hook events** that give authoritative status (working/idle/waiting) — far better than any output-watching heuristic.

I'm flagging this honestly because you said don't tell you no. I'm not. I'm telling you the heuristic from the Trenches spec ("saw output in last 2s") is the *worse* approach now that hook events exist, and we should use them instead. That's a small change that makes the build genuinely robust the first time.

You also said this should be one tool, fun name, half an hour with Claude Code. Let's make it Claude Code's first try, not "Claude Code iterating for two days because the spec was loose."

Here's the plan in one document. It's the prompt you'll paste.

Drop that file into Claude Code at the root of a fresh `sequoias` repo. It's self-contained — no follow-up questions Claude Code will reasonably need to ask before the test plan in §9 passes.

A few things worth knowing about the choices I made, in case you want to push back before pasting:

**The big honest finding** — `claude-control` (sverrirsig on GitHub) is doing 80% of what you described, MIT-licensed, Next.js, Mac-only, uses Claude Code's hook events and `gh` integration. It auto-discovers running claude processes via the process table, classifies sessions as Working/Idle/Waiting/Errored using real-time hook events with CPU/JSONL heuristic fallback, shows git changes and PR status, and handles worktree creation. If your goal is "use it now," install that and go. If your goal is "build the prototype that informs Garrison's Workspace primitive," then build Sequoias. I assumed the latter, but if I assumed wrong, that's a 30-second decision.

**I changed one thing from your spec** — Trenches uses an output-watching heuristic for idle detection. The Claude Code team shipped real hook events since then (`UserPromptSubmit`, `Stop`, `Notification`). Using hooks instead of output-sniffing makes it robust the first time. The prompt installs hooks on startup and restores `settings.json` on shutdown — test #11 verifies the restoration works.

**I dropped iTerm tab management from the v1.** You floated iTerm with renamed tabs as one option and xterm.js in a web UI as another. Both are valid; trying to do both at once is what kills "half an hour" projects. The web UI route reuses Harmonica's xterm.js + node-pty pattern almost verbatim, so it's the lower-risk path.

**The 14 Playwright tests are the contract.** They cover every behavior that matters: port stability, hook-driven status, env rewriting per-file scope, PR creation, archive cleanup, hook installation/removal, and persistence across restart. If Claude Code claims done without them passing, push back. If it tries to skip the hook-cleanup test (#11) because "it's hard," push back harder — leaving stray hooks in your `~/.claude/settings.json` is exactly the kind of papercut that ruins this kind of tool.

**One more thing to decide before you paste.** I assumed the IDE launch flag is `--ide rebased` (a CLI command name). If `rebased` doesn't have a CLI launcher set up, you'll need to either run JetBrains' "Create Command-line Launcher" once on your machine, or pass an absolute path like `--ide /Applications/Rebased.app/Contents/MacOS/rebased`. Both work with the spec as written.

So I was thinking about Garrison here and the future of Garrison. Garrison is aimed at building agents and how to make them useful, how to make them work for us. Recently I've started working on an area there, which is the tools area, where we have some tools in Garrison that, in my case, are good for development. They make more sense when working in this new era where agentic coding is king than for traditional software development, right? 
So what I'm thinking is that probably there were other open source projects that existed before agentic coding that had a similar spirit of being a source of truth where people could find good tools for their area. I don't mean just the software development area but for any business area, operations area, marketing, financial data, several different areas. Now those tools are different and one thing is that now we can just build our own tool and that's why I did ekoa because we can just build our own tools and adjust them to our workflow, to the way we work, to our needs. Before we had to find the tools, use what was out there even if we didn't like it too much, even if it wasn't very good. Now that's much better.

I'm thinking we should try to find out how this problem was solved before, by sharing the good tools, having a good repo for that kind of stuff, and which ones became successful. I mean curated lists that also allow seeing quickly if it makes sense to us, how it works, and if it's well rated. Is important but it's not the only important thing. It's important to figure out if it works for us and if it is the best tool for us or not, I would like you to do some research on these kinds of projects that existed before. Let's think about a way to bring this to Garrison somehow, not maybe for that operatives part, those primitives, because that's kind of nailed down already, but to the tooling part, to link around software development, with gentle coding in my case, but that could work for other areas as well.

```
;
    } catch (err) {

  if (cmd === "add") {
    const [, id, cron, ...rest] = argv;
    if (!id || !cron || rest.length === 0) {
      console.error("usage: scheduler.mjs add <id> <cron> <command...>");
      return 2;
    }
    try { parseCron(cron); }
    catch (err) { console.error(`invalid cron: ${err.message}`); return 1; }
    const command = rest.join(" ");
    const jobs = await loadJobs();
    const next = jobs.filter((j) => j.id !== id);
    next.push({ id, cron, command, enabled: true });
    await saveJobs(next);
    console.log(`added ${id}`);
    return 0;
  }

  if (cmd === "remove") {
    const id = argv[1];
    if (!id) { console.error("usage: scheduler.mjs remove <id>"); return 2; }
    const jobs = await loadJobs();
    const next = jobs.filter((j) => j.id !== id);
    await saveJobs(next);
    console.log(`removed ${id} (was ${jobs.length - next.length === 1 ? "present" : "absent"})`);
    return 0;
  }

  if (cmd === "run-now") {

    if (!id) { console.error("usage: scheduler.mjs run-now <id>"); return 2; }
    const jobs = await loadJobs();
    const job = jobs.find((j) => j.id === id);
    if (!job) { console.error(`job not found: ${id}`); return 1; }
    const result = await runJob(job);
    console.log(`ran ${id} exit=${result.exit}`);
    return result.exit === 0 ? 0 : 1;
  }

  if (cmd === "tick") {
    const ran = await tick();
    process.stdout.write(JSON.stringify({ ran }) + "\n");
    return 0;
  }

  if (cmd === "daemon") {
    await daemon();
    return 0;
```

>  I've got enough material to work with now. Let me figure out how to frame this for Goncalo—he's asking about how tool discovery worked before agentic systems, what patterns succeeded, and how we might apply those lessons to Garrison's tooling layer, particularly around Skills and Fittings rather than the lower-level Operatives. I need to connect this back to Garrison's five capability kinds and think through what the parallel would be.
> 
> From my research, I'm seeing three major discovery patterns emerge: community-curated lists like Awesome Lists that rely on editorial judgment and GitHub's decentralized nature, package registries like npm and PyPI that serve as authoritative sources but struggle with search and rely on external signals like download counts and GitHub stars, and IDE marketplaces like VS Code that embed discovery directly into the tool itself.
> 
> Then there are cross-product comparison sites like StackShare and G2 that show what companies use, though they're often compromised by paid placement, plus the older directory model that failed once search engines became good enough. Now I'm seeing a new wave of AI-specific discovery platforms emerging—multiple competing sites trying to catalog Claude plugins and MCP tools.
> 
> Beyond just ratings, what actually matters is documentation quality, how actively maintained the tool is, whether the maintainer responds to issues, whether it's a single person or a team, test coverage, who else depends on it, security considerations, and how well it integrates with existing systems.
> 
> The real problem Goncalo flagged is that ratings measure average satisfaction, not whether a tool fits *your* specific workflow—things like philosophical alignment or stack assumptions don't fit on a 1-5 scale. The most valuable signals come from seeing what similar users are running or just trying the tool yourself for a few minutes. MCPfinder's approach of having an agent match tools to your context in real-time is the breakthrough here.
> 
> For Garrison's strategy, the key is to avoid building yet another marketplace and instead embed discovery where people already are.
> 
> The real advantage Garrison has is an Operative that understands your entire composition—it can search registries on your behalf with full context about what you're running and what you're trying to accomplish. That's the agentic version of StackShare's peer evidence, but with runtime visibility that MCPfinder doesn't have. The curation models that actually work are editorial curation like awesome lists, in-place installation with ratings like VS Code, or peer evidence like StackShare—pure rating aggregators and directories without installation friction tend to struggle.
> 
> Since Skills have structured descriptors that explain what they do and what they expect, the Operative can compute actual fit scores rather than just popularity metrics. For Garrison specifically, I'm thinking about starting with a curated Awesome Garrison Skills repo as a low-friction entry point, then eventually building a Skills directory site that layers on curated tags, composition usage data, install commands, and fit notes for different workflows—essentially a PulseMCP equivalent for Garrison.
> 
> The real differentiation would be an agentic skill finder built into Garrison itself, letting the Operative search and evaluate fit mid-conversation, plus stack fingerprints showing which skills and orchestrators commonly run together. Meanwhile, Ekoa handles the broader business-area tooling while Garrison stays focused on what Operatives actually compose with.
> 
> Looking at what made curation work elsewhere: low-friction contribution paths, visible social proof, and an opinionated maintainer keeping standards high. What consistently failed was rating manipulation, paid placement without an install mechanism, and no editorial oversight.
> 
> I'm thinking through how to structure this into a concrete response—the user wants substantive design thinking, not generalization, and they'd rather see scoped briefs than code artifacts. I'm settling on a prose-heavy approach with minimal structure since that matches how the user thinks, and I'll skip the preamble to get straight into the substantive research and brainstorming they're asking for.

Three big eras of "how did people find good tools" before agentic coding, each with distinct lessons.

**The awesome-list era (2014–present).** Sindre Sorhus started it with `awesome` in 2014 — a curated, opinionated markdown list, PR-driven, with an explicit editorial bar: "only awesome items, awesome lists are curations of the best, not everything." It went viral because the contract was tiny (a `README.md` in a git repo) and the social proof was just GitHub stars. The pattern that won: low-friction contribution, opinionated maintainers, no UI to build. The pattern that failed: lists without an editorial bar that turned into dumps. The real innovation wasn't the list — it was the cultural rule that "awesome" was an emotional measure of delight, and stuff that didn't clear that bar got rejected.

**The directory/aggregator era (StackShare, AlternativeTo, G2, Capterra, Slant, ProductHunt, SaaSHub).** These tried to scale curation with reviews, ratings, "alternatives to X" pages, and stack graphs. Two real ideas survived. First, AlternativeTo's "alternatives to" framing — people don't search "best note app," they search "alternatives to Notion." Discovery is comparative, not absolute. Second, StackShare's killer move: showing actual stacks of real companies. "Airbnb runs Postgres + Rails + Redis" is more persuasive than 4.7 stars from anonymous reviewers. The failure mode was monetization: G2 acquired Capterra/GetApp/Software Advice, vendors pay for visibility, gift-card-for-review programs erode trust. Paid placement is the death of curation.

**The platform-marketplace era (npm, VS Code Marketplace, Sublime Package Control, Obsidian community plugins, JetBrains plugins).** The platform owns discovery, install is one click, signals are install count + rating + last-updated + maintainer. Obsidian is interesting: every community plugin gets manually reviewed before listing — that's the editorial bar moved into the platform itself. VS Code is interesting in the opposite direction: open submission, signals do the curation. Both work, for different reasons.

**Where the agentic ecosystem is now.** Worth noticing: this exact problem is being re-solved live for Claude Code and MCP, and the structure has split into three layers that all coexist:

1. *Authoritative registry* — `registry.modelcontextprotocol.io`, npm itself, the APM registry. Machine-readable, no curation, source of truth for "does this exist."
2. *Curated directories* — PulseMCP (hand-reviewed), claudemarketplaces.com, buildwithclaude.com, awesome-claude-plugins. Human editorial bar on top of the registry.
3. *Agent-native discovery* — MCPfinder is the one to study. It's an MCP server whose only job is to find other MCP servers. The agent itself searches the registry mid-conversation, inspects trust signals, generates the install snippet. Discovery happens inline with the work, not in a separate browse-and-evaluate session.

That third layer is the new thing, and it's where Garrison has an unfair advantage nobody in the historical lineage had: **the Operative already knows the composition**. It knows what's installed, what kinds are wired together, what the user is actually trying to do. "Fit" — the thing you correctly identified as the missing axis beyond ratings — is computable from the composition graph plus the skill's manifest, in a way it never was for "should I pick Mongo or Postgres."

**What this suggests for Garrison's tooling layer**, in rough order of cost:

- *Awesome-Garrison-Skills repo.* Cheapest possible move, established cultural pattern, no infra. One opinionated maintainer (you), PRs welcome, explicit bar: "this skill must be one I'd actually install." Establishes vocabulary and proves there's a community before you build anything. This is what every successful ecosystem did first.
- *Stack fingerprints / co-installation graph.* Once enough Garrisons exist, "compositions that include this skill also include X" becomes a signal you literally cannot fake — it's what made StackShare valuable. Privacy-respecting (anonymized, opt-in) but extremely high-signal.
- *A discovery Skill (or Faculty surface) inside Garrison itself.* The MCPfinder pattern, applied to the Fittings Registry. The Operative can search, read manifests, check fit against the current composition, propose an install. This is the move only Garrison can make, and it leans directly into the project's transparency thesis — instead of a black-box recommendation, the Operative shows its reasoning.
- *A web directory eventually* (PulseMCP-style for Garrison). Don't build this until the awesome-list and the agent-side discovery have proven the demand. Web directories without an install path die; with an install path, they're VS Code Marketplace.

Two things I'd flag as traps. First, don't build broad-domain discovery (marketing tools, financial tools) inside Garrison — that's the multi-domain complexity you already pushed to Ekoa, and it'd pull the project off-thesis. Keep Garrison's tooling discovery scoped to what an Operative can actually consume: skills, automation runners, memory stores. Second, resist building rating UI early. Ratings without volume are noise, and once you have rating UI you're committed to fighting gaming. Co-installation and editorial curation give you most of the signal at none of the cost.

Want me to turn any of these into a brief — most natural candidate is the awesome-list + the discovery-Skill pair, since those are the two cheapest moves and they reinforce each other.

Stations keeps coming back as strong because each tool really is a distinct dashboard or workspace you navigate to, like battle stations. Posts and Kit are solid too, but Stations seems to align best with how he's actually thinking about the structure.
> 
> Now I'm realizing the core distinction: Operatives are agents that *do things*, while Tools are instruments you *use*. A soldier is an Operative, but their radio, compass, and sights are the equipment they operate. I want to find a name that captures that relationship — something that signals "this is a piece of equipment or workstation you interact with" rather than an autonomous entity. I'm leaning toward suggesting a few strong candidates and letting him choose which resonates most. ts**? "Command post," "observation post," "listening post." Each Tool is essentially a post — a station where you go to do a particular kind of work. "I'm at my worktree post," "switching to the session post." It also has that "fixed position you can man or leave" feel that matches his switching-tools behavior.
> 
> Or **Bays**? A bay in a hangar/garage is a dedicated workspace. "Sequoia's worktree bay, sessions bay, terminal bay." Hm, decent.
> 
> **Stations** is probably most accessible. "Battle stations." "Workstation." "Charging station." Each Garrison Tool is a station for a specific kind of work.
> 
> I'll suggest a few and recommend one.
> 
> Now I'm thinking through how the Tools architecture should parallel the Operatives structure. Operatives have five capability kinds, around thirteen or fourteen Faculties that form the UX taxonomy, and Fittings that implement those Faculties concretely — all wired together through provides/consumes relationships. For Tools, I'd want something similar but simpler: maybe just one or two base capability kinds like a UI surface and data source, a set of Faculties that users can extend with their own, and the same Fittings and wiring pattern.
> 
> The key insight is that Operatives' five kinds are runtime concepts describing how things behave, whereas Tools are more like UI panels. So Tools might need just three kinds: a panel for the main UI surface, a provider to expose data that other panels consume, and an action that's callable by other panels or Operatives. That gives structure without being overly rigid.

The Sequoias example shows how this works in practice: a worktree-creation panel provides a stream of current worktrees, a sessions panel consumes that stream and provides session state, and a terminal panel sits alongside them. Each panel is wired through its Faculty to know what it can consume and provide.
> 
> For Operatives, Faculties are fixed because the runtime depends on them, but for Tools they're user-defined — though really what matters is the contract between what a Tool provides and what it consumes. So the model is lighter than it first seemed: a Tool declares its inputs and outputs with whatever schema names make sense, the wiring graph connects them, and Faculty becomes more of a UI organizational concept.

>

That points toward location or equipment language — Stations feels cleanest for accessibility, though Posts has stronger thematic alignment with the Garrison military aesthetic.
> 
> For structure, I should sketch out how this parallel area mirrors Operatives: same card layout, same filtering and search, same ability to organize and manage, but focused on tools instead of characters. Then I can address how Sequoias decompose within this framework — whether they're tool-level or workspace-level entities.
> 
> On the naming front, I'm leaning toward something that signals "tools" without being too literal. "Arsenal" feels right — it's got the sense of a curated collection, it's got some personality, and it parallels the Operatives framing nicely.
> 
> The key tension I'm noticing: he's right that tools don't need the same kind of contract as Operative faculties, but they absolutely need *some* contract — specifically around what they produce and consume. His Sequoias example actually proves this: the worktree creator outputs something that the sessions view needs as input. So the contract isn't about behavior or interface shape, it's about data flow. That's worth pushing back on gently.
> 
> Without that contract, the tools area is just a disconnected tab of mini-apps. With it, you get real IDE-level integration where tools can actually wire together and compose. That's the difference between a feature and a platform.

Got it — I was answering the wrong question. The interesting move isn't a discovery layer for the world; it's a **second top-level area inside Garrison** that parallels Operatives, holds the non-agentic (or quasi-agentic) tools you actually use day-to-day, and uses the same Faculty/Fitting wiring spirit so you can swap parts in and out without rewriting anything. The discovery/curation stuff can come back later as a downstream consequence — first this has to be useful for you.

**On the name.** Artillery doesn't quite fit — too combat-coded for what's basically your workshop. A few options that play nicer with the Garrison metaphor:

- **Stations** — battle stations, workstations, charging stations. Reads as "a place I go to do a kind of work." Maps directly to your own language ("an area for creating worktrees, an area for sessions").
- **Posts** — command post, observation post, listening post. More military-flavored, fits Garrison, and has the right "fixed position you man or leave" feel that matches your tool-switching habit.
- **Bays** — workshop/hangar bays. Technical, neutral, accurate.

I'd lean **Stations**. It's the most natural in plain English, and it handles both senses: a station is a *location* (the UI panel) and a *function* (the role it plays).

**On the structure.** I think the parallel to Operatives works, but with a deliberately thinner spine:

- Operatives have 5 fixed runtime kinds because the runtime depends on it. Stations don't have that constraint — they're closer to UI surfaces with data and actions attached. So instead of a locked vocabulary, maybe just three roles a Station Fitting can play: **panel** (renders a UI surface), **provider** (exposes data/state others can read), **action** (exposes a callable other panels or Operatives can invoke). Most Stations will be all three.
- Faculties stay as the UX/composer taxonomy, same as Operatives — but the seed set is small and the set is **open**. You ship with well-known Faculties (`worktree-management`, `session-view`, `terminal`, `screen-share`, `browser`, `whiteboard`, ...) that define stable provides/consumes contracts. Users can declare ad-hoc Faculties in their `x-garrison` block when they need something one-off.
- The wiring is the part you don't want to give up. "Tools that fit my workflow" is exactly the property the provides/consumes graph buys you — a Station can be replaced with a different Fitting in the same Faculty slot, and the rest of the graph keeps working. Without that, the area is just a tab full of unrelated mini-apps, which is the thing you've been trying to escape.

**Sequoias as the worked example.** What you described basically decomposes into three Stations:

- A **worktree-management** Station that *provides* the current set of worktrees and *exposes an action* to create new ones with your preferred ports/commands.
- A **session-view** Station that *consumes* that worktree stream and *provides* session state (what's running, idle, needs attention, finished). Actions: open PR, kill, refocus.
- A **terminal** Station (something xterm-flavored, already in the roadmap) that consumes a worktree selection and renders a terminal in that directory.

That decomposition does the thing you keep wanting: when your worktree workflow changes, you swap the worktree-management Fitting and the sessions/terminal panels keep humming. When a new terminal Fitting comes along, same. And you stop building Sequoias-the-monolith and start adding to Garrison-the-shell — which is the right ratchet.

**The Operative bridge, deferred but worth scoping.** Once Stations expose actions through the same provides/consumes contract Operatives already use, the agent-skill kind can invoke them just like any other tool. "Verity, spin up a worktree for the Garrison-Stations brief and open a terminal in it" becomes a one-liner. You don't need this on day one, but designing the action contract now so it's the same shape an Operative would call later costs nothing extra and unlocks everything.

Each Fitting is independent enough that swapping one out doesn't break the others—the wiring contract stays intact. > 
> The core issue is that standalone tools like Sequoias work for one workflow snapshot but break when the workflow changes, and they don't compose with each other or Garrison's existing primitives, leading to duplicated capability. The solution is to integrate tooling directly into Garrison using the same Faculty/Fitting architecture that already powers Operatives, so tools become another composable layer rather than a separate system.
> 
> I'm settling on "Armory" as the Faculty name — it maps naturally to the Garrison metaphor as the place where gear lives and pairs well with Operatives. Now I need to define what the Fitting contract looks like for tools living under the Armory.
> 
> A Fitting can take on three roles: it can render UI as a panel, expose data or operations through the provides/consumes graph, or expose callable actions. Most useful ones will do all three, and the wiring mechanism is the same as the rest of Garrison. The Armory Faculty itself is open and extensible — Garrison ships with a seed set of stable Fittings like worktree management and terminal, but users can also define their own ad-hoc Armory Fittings in their composition.
> 
> Ad-hoc Fittings trade off composition guarantees for flexibility when you need something custom. The Garrison shell itself handles the Armory dynamically by reading the active composition's configuration, identifying which Fittings belong to Armory faculties, rendering their panels, and wiring their providers and actions into the graph — the same pattern used elsewhere in the system without any special-case UI.
> 
> For the verification target, I'm decomposing Sequoias from a single Next.js app into three Fittings across different faculties: one handles worktree management and provides the current set while exposing an action to create new worktrees with custom port allocation and startup.
> 
> The second Fitting consumes that worktree stream to track session state and expose actions like opening PRs or killing sessions, while the third renders an xterm-based terminal for the active worktree. The key benefit is that when the worktree workflow changes—different ports, startup commands, or VCS layout—only the worktree-management Fitting needs replacement; the others keep working because they depend on the contract, not the implementation details.
> 
> I'm thinking about how to bridge this with agent skills later: if Armory Fittings use the same action contract that Operatives already use, then an agent-skill Operative could invoke an Armory tool action through the same wiring graph—something like "spin up a worktree and open a terminal" as a single orchestrated command. This bridge isn't a v1 priority, but designing the action contract this way now costs nothing and keeps the door open for later without rework.
> 
> For now, I'm scoping out public discovery, curated lists, and marketplaces—those are downstream consequences of having something useful, not prerequisites. The focus is getting Garrison working well for a single user first.
> 
> I'm also keeping multi-domain tooling out of scope; that belongs in Ekoa. The Armory stays focused on tools that actually fit into an agentic-development workflow.
> 
> On the roadmap side, the planned "Tools area" becomes Armory faculty with seed Fittings, screen-share moves into the Armory as a Fitting, and Sequoias' three-Fitting decomposition becomes the verification milestone—once Garrison can host all three and Sequoias can be retired.

> 
> What stays the same: the five capability kinds for Operatives, the composer/UX taxonomy, the provides/consumes wiring contract, and the APM-based packaging with the x-garrison block. ### human — 2026-05-11 12:33 UTC

So here's a little bit more framing for the Garrison project so that when we're building the readme for the open source project and the web page to support the project, we kind of start with this idea. People will think, "Why Garrison? Why not just use OpenClaw or Hermes or whatever everybody else is using?" The answer is that with OpenClaw and Hermes other people are making the decisions for you on how it works. Garrison is completely customizable. It's a thin layer for running the several components that AI agents need and lets you choose and pick and choose what to use. It offers all the same functionality that the other agents offer but customizable by you. Also it's transparent: all the layers because it only has a thin layer, everything it does is visible to you, is auditable, and it's easily understandable because most of the components are based on natural language like skills and that kind of stuff.

One of the use cases is that you are building your own agent. You know, you're not depending on the decisions of other people on how you should do things and that's very good especially for people that know what they're doing and have been doing this for a while. For people like me, for example, I'm a software developer. I don't use OpenClaw and I don't use Hermes. I don't use any of those because they do too much that I don't want them to do. They are too opaque. They are very autonomous but autonomous in a way that sometimes it doesn't work for what I want it to work and other times it does too much.

The components in Garrison allow you to take a progressive approach and it starts with maybe not automating everything you want out of the box. What it automates, it automates in a way that you've chosen, you've chosen for it to be automatable. Also if you're building commercial agents, you can build agents with this platform that companies are going to adhere to because everything is visible. You can add layers of governance if you want and you can tweak every aspect of your agent/operative with Garrison.

I think these framings are important because we need to be very clear in the beginning because there's a lot of hype still around stuff like Hermes, OpenClaw, nano claw, all these things around that came after OpenClaw that people are still very excited about. It's going to be difficult to explain why we're building Garrison. Why not just take OpenClaw, install it, or Hermes, install it, and work with it? These are the two reasons, or the three reasons. I think there are three reasons actually:
1. For people that know what they're doing in this space and they want more control
2. For people that want to understand what's happening and want to choose and customize everything it does according to their needs instead of relying on other people's opinions and on how they should do things
3. For people that can create agents that
 For people that can create agents that are deployable to businesses because they are transparent, they can understand everything that happens, they are controllable, and they are progressive. The progress is in the more they are used and the better we choose the tools to use it with 

One thing that we also must make clear is that APM, Microsoft came up with APM for this reason: people need to be able to create agents in secure, transparent ways with some guidance. Creating agents is something new so even people from software development sometimes misunderstand and have difficulties understanding all the primitives that are needed to build a proper agent with good access, to build the tooling around it to be able to make it work well, to communicate with it easily, and to have the tooling if people want to work remotely, if they want to work on the computer, if they want to work with a UI, if they want to add things. Composable and that's why Microsoft saw that, that's why they came up with APM. With just a few days of coming out, it already has thousands of stars on GitHub and it's very popular because it's supposed to replace NPM, which was for traditional software development but for agents. We must include a line about APM and about how garrison aligns with this vision of APM

Got it. The positioning crystallizes into one thesis: *other platforms decide for you; Garrison lets you decide.* Same functionality, but you compose it. A thin, transparent layer where every piece is visible, auditable, and mostly natural-language — not a black box being autonomous in ways you didn't ask for.

The three audiences this targets:

1. **Practitioners** who already know how they want their agents to behave and find OpenClaw/Hermes too opinionated, too autonomous-in-the-wrong-direction, too much out of the box.
2. **The understand-and-customize crowd** — people who don't want someone else's defaults baked into their stack, and who'd rather progressively choose what gets automated than inherit it.
3. **Builders of commercial/deployable agents** — because transparency, governance hooks, and the ability to tweak every layer are what make an agent something a company will actually adopt.

The APM line writes itself: Microsoft built APM because creating agents is genuinely new — even seasoned developers underestimate the primitives involved (skills, access, channels, memory, orchestration), and there was no NPM-equivalent for that world. Thousands of GitHub stars in days proves the gap was real. Garrison is what you build *on top of* APM — the thin runtime that takes APM's composable package model and turns it into running Operatives you fully control.

One thing worth deciding before drafting: the README and the landing page want different registers. README is for developers landing on the repo (technical, "what is this thing, why should I clone it"). Landing page is for prospects browsing (benefit-led, with the comparison framing front and center). Want me to draft both, or start with one — and if one, which?

Side note on terminology: "OpenClaw" and "Hermes" — are those the actual names you want to use in public copy, or speech-to-text stand-ins for OpenCode and something else? They've been consistent across our sessions so I've left them alone, but public-facing copy is where it matters to get the names exactly right.

```
# Agent Garrison Orchestrator

You are the behavior spine for a local Agent Garrison operative.
Coordinate installed Faculties, respect configured guardrails, report every meaningful action, and verify before claiming success.

## Operating discipline

- Be concise. State the result first; details follow only if useful.
- Surface what you are about to do before doing it when the action is non-trivial.
- If a request is ambiguous, ask one focused question rather than guessing.
- If you cannot complete something, say so directly and explain what's blocking you.

## Reply contract

End every reply with the following token on its own line:

    [orchestrator-active]

This is a verification marker proving this prompt reached the model. Do not omit it, even on short replies.

#!/usr/bin/env node
// Scheduler for Garrison.
// Stdlib-only. Cron grammar covers the patterns Phase 2 needs:
// "*", "*/N", single values, comma lists, and ranges (a-b) across all
// 5 fields. No '@yearly' style aliases, no seconds field.
//
// Usage:
//   node scheduler.mjs --probe                  # health check, prints "ok"
//   node scheduler.mjs list                     # JSON list of jobs
//   node scheduler.mjs add <id> <cron> <cmd>    # add or replace a job
//   node scheduler.mjs remove <id>  
… [truncated]
```

```
fittings/seed/scheduler/scripts/scheduler.mjs
import fs from "node:fs/promises";
import fsSync from "node:fs";
import path from "node:path";
import { spawn } from "node:child_process";

const JOBS_FILE = process.env.GARRISON_SCHEDULER_JOBS
  ?? path.resolve(process.cwd(), "data/scheduler-jobs.json");
const LOG_FILE = process.env.GARRISON_SCHEDULER_LOG
  ?? path.resolve(process.cwd(), "data/scheduler.log");
const TICK_INTERVAL_MS = 60_000;

async function loadJobs() {
  try {
    const raw = await fs.readFile(JOBS_FILE, "utf8");
    const parsed = JSON.parse(raw);
    if (!Array.isArray(parsed)) throw new Error("jobs file is not an array");
    return parsed;
  } catch (err) {
    if (err.code === "ENOENT") return [];
    throw err;
  }
}

async function saveJobs(jobs) {
  await fs.mkdir(path.dirname(JOBS_FILE), { recursive: true });
  await fs.writeFile(JOBS_FILE, JSON.stringify(jobs, null, 2) + "\n");
}

function parseCron(cron) {
  const parts = cron.trim().split(/\s+/);
  if (parts.length !== 5) {
    throw new Error(`cron must have 5 fields, got ${parts.length}: "${cron}"`);
  }
  const ranges = [
    [0, 59], // minute
    [0, 23], // hour
    [1, 31], // day of month
    [1, 12], // month
    [0, 6]   // day of week
  ];
  return parts.map((spec, i) => parseField(spec, ranges[i][0], ranges[i][1], `field ${i}`));
}

function parseField(spec, lo, hi, label) {
  if (spec === "*") return { kind: "any" };
  if (spec.startsWith("*/")) {
    const step = Number(spec.slice(2));
    if (!Number.isInteger(step) || step <= 0) {
      throw new Error(`${label}: invalid step "${spec}"`);
    }
    return { kind: "step", step, lo };
  }
  const values = new Set();
  for (const part of spec.split(",")) {
    if (part.includes("-")) {
      const [a, b] = part.split("-").map(Number);
      if (!Number.isInteger(a) || !Number.isInteger(b) || a < lo || b > hi || a > b) {
        throw new Error(`${label}: invalid range "${part}"`);
      }
      for (let v = a; v <= b; v++) val
… [truncated]
```

```
// Scheduler for Garrison.
// Stdlib-only. Cron grammar covers the patterns Phase 2 needs:
// "*", "*/N", single values, comma lists, and ranges (a-b) across all
// 5 fields. No '@yearly' style aliases, no seconds field.
//
// Usage:
//   node scheduler.mjs --probe                  # health check, prints "ok"
//   node scheduler.mjs list                     # JSON list of jobs
//   node scheduler.mjs add <id> <cron> <cmd>    # add or replace a job
//   node scheduler.mjs remove <id>  
… [truncated]
```

A few notes on choices I made:

- **Kept "progressive automation"** as a named concept rather than just describing it — you used the phrase clearly and it's a useful counterweight to the "too autonomous" complaint about OpenClaw/Hermes.
- **APM paragraph is short and factual**, not breathless. Mentioning the thousands of stars is fine as evidence the gap was real, but I framed it as a foundation Garrison aligns with, not as a hype cosign.
- **Told Claude Code to name OpenClaw and Hermes directly** in the Why Garrison section. Refusing to name them would weaken the contrast — you're not insulting them, you're explaining a design choice.
- **Verification step at the end** as you prefer, with specific things to check (terminology consistency, no Monstropolis leftovers, honest Status section).
- **No code from me in the brief itself** — it's pure instructions for Claude Code to execute against the actual file.

One thing worth flagging: the brief instructs Claude Code to keep the Status section honest about cross-session memory and the runtime SDK being deferred. If the current README is more aspirational than that, this will be a visible shift. Worth it for a public-facing repo, but flagging in case you want to soften.

```
DECISIONS.md
**Source:** Terminology doc; Phase 1 of the consolidated v1 plan.
**Status:** Settled.

## 2026-05-04 · `src/components/` directory retained intentionally

The `src/components/` directory keeps the word "component" because in
that path it refers to React components, not to Garrison Fittings.
Future "remove all `component` references" passes must NOT rename it.
**Source:** consolidated v1 plan §3.1 ("NOT where they refer to React
components — those stay `Component`"). **Status:** Settled.

## 2026-05-04 · `component_shape` YAML field name retained

The `x-garrison.component_shape` YAML field was not renamed to
`fitting_shape` even though TypeScript identifiers (`fittingShapes`,
`FittingShape`) were. Per plan §3.1, "the YAML field name itself stays
... don't churn manifest schemas for cosmetic gain." Same reasoning
applies to `cardinality_hint`.
**Source:** consolidated v1 plan §3.1. **Status:** Settled.

## 2026-05-04 · testing-framework Faculty renamed to skills, purpose broadened

The Faculty at slot 6 is renamed from `testing-framework` to `skills`
with a broader purpose: reusable capabilities the Operative can invoke,
of which test authoring is one valid shape. The parser accepts the
deprecated `testing-framework` value with a console warning.
**Source:** consolidated v1 plan Phase 4 (§6). **Status:** Settled.

## 2026-05-04 · License pending decision between MIT and Apache-2.0

The v1 release license is not formally chosen. `CONTRIBUTING.md` lists
MIT as the working default; no `LICENSE` file is committed because
adding one is the decision. Choose before the first external Fitting
submission.
**Source:** consolidated v1 plan §15 (decision points to surface).
**Status:** Open.

The text of the Honesty Test in [GOVERNANCE.md](./GOVERNANCE.md) §3 is
drafted from references in the consolidated v1 plan, not copied
verbatim from the canonical Governance doc (which was not available
```

```
AGENTS.md
6. Hitting **Run** on a configured composition:
   - Calls `apm install` and reports each step in the live log.
   - Materialises `.env` from vault into the composition directory.
   - Assembles orchestrator+soul system prompt and starts a Claude Code session with it.
   - Every Fitting's `x-garrison.verify` hook passes.
   - Logs stream live to the Run tab.
7. Closing the browser tab and reopening shows the Operative still running with log scrollback.
8. **Stop** kills processes cleanly, wipes materialised `.env`, reports stopped.
9. **Dev mode** watches a local-path Fitting; editing a file in that Fitting triggers `apm install` + Operative restart within ~10 s.
10. At least one seed Fitting ships a UI extension that renders inside its Faculty's tab when installed.

Capability wiring (added in v1 consolidated milestone):

11. Every selected Fitting's `provides`/`consumes` resolves via the capability resolver before Compose marks ready. Errors surface in the readiness panel.
12. The validation pipeline (`tsx scripts/validate-fitting.ts <path>`) passes for every seed Fitting.

---

## 11. Roadmap

### v1 — dogfood-ready (defined above)

### v1.1

- **Workspace Faculty**: pane Fittings (terminal-pane attached to a Gateway session, browser-pane, log-tail, file-explorer-pane) + a layout config. Enables the multi-pane dev-workspace experience some users want as a replacement for proprietary desktop agent UIs.
- Additional platform targets via APM's multi-target install (Codex, etc.).
- Fitting issue-based submission flow (manual accept).
- Ratings UI.
- UI-kit published as its own APM package (design tokens + small React primitive set).

### Runtime SDK milestone

- Reference orchestrator Fitting (closes the gap noted in `fittings/seed/README.md`).
- The `Runtime` interface: `consume()`, events, lifecycle.
- Pattern A — synchronous sub-agent invocation for orchestrator → agent-skill.
- AI-driven validators (security, prompt-injection) replace the v1 placeholder
… [truncated]
```


```
AGENTS.md
Stop closes everything cleanly. Dev mode watches local-path Fittings and re-applies on save.

**Tech stack is not pinned.** v1 should be implementable by whichever coding agent the user prefers. Default suggestion: Next.js 14 + TypeScript + Tailwind for the web app, since it gives a single-process full-stack story with no auth — but anything that delivers the §10 Definition of Done is acceptable.

---

## 2. Faculties (13 + derived)

Each Faculty has a **cardinality** (single / multi) and a closed set of accepted **Fitting shapes**. Tasks is *derived*. Workspace is reserved for v1.1 (see §11).

| # | Faculty | Cardinality | Fitting shapes | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heartbeat | single | script, skill, system prompt, manual instructions | The cadence that wakes the Operative. Cron-style, loop-style, scheduled-task-style. Only loop variants are programmatic; the rest are manual instructions. |
| 2 | Scheduler | single | script, skill | Anything outside the heartbeat cadence. |
| 3 | Data sources | multi | MCP, CLI | Integration-flavoured. One-way fetch in v1; two-way sync later. |
| 4 | Knowledge base | multi | skill, MCP, script | Docs/codebases/refs the Operative can *read*. Distinct from data sources (live API) and memory (self-learned). |
| 5 | Automations | multi | CLI+skill, MCP | Things the Operative can *do* in the world: drive a browser, control a desktop, run scripted UI flows. |
| 6 | Skills | multi | script, skill | Reusable agent skills the Operative can invoke during work — including but not limited to test authoring. Renamed from `testing-framework` in v1. |
| 7 | Memory | single | skill, system prompt, hook | What the Operative knows — both within-session and across sessions. A single Memory Fitting decides how it handles both. Config space includes things like recency window, persistence cadence, and where compiled memory is written. |
| 8 | Classifier | single | skill, system prompt | Decides what to do with each prompt. Tier mode
… [truncated]
```

```
<chat_summary>**Conversation Overview**

Gonçalo, who is building the Ekoa platform, introduced a new sister project called AWC (Agentic Workflow Compositions) — a free, community-editable website where users compose autonomous agent setups by selecting components across a set of defined primitives, then download a zip containing an `instructions.md` file that their chosen CLI platform (initially Claude Code) reads and executes to install the full environment. The project is motivated by Gonçalo's observation that autonomous development platforms like OpenClaw and Hermes are opaque black boxes; AWC aims to make the underlying concepts (heartbeat, classifier, gateway, memory, etc.) transparent, composable, and verifiable. Gonçalo plans to use AWC himself for ongoing workflow creation and eventually open it to the broader community.

The conversation moved through a detailed planning session covering the full architecture. Claude proposed an initial structure and Gonçalo refined it iteratively. Key decisions included: defining 14 primitives (Heartbeat, Scheduler, Data Sources, Knowledge Base, Automations, Testing Framework, Memory Immediate, Memory Persistent, Classifier, Gateway, Channels, Observability, Soul, and Orchestrator), with the Orchestrator designated as the governing primitive that owns global config; establishing a closed set of component types (script, agent instructions, manual instructions, plugin, skill, hook, system prompt); using GitHub as the storage backbone with fork-style versioning; and producing a zip output containing `instructions.md`, `uninstall.md`, component files, scripts, and baked `.env` files. Gonçalo accepted suggestions for Observability and Knowledge Base primitives, renamed the identity primitive to "Soul," and folded approval gates into a permissions mode config option (full-auto, au
… [truncated]
```

```
<chat url='https://claude.ai/chat/29ec0d93-7f75-411f-8f2e-022a6e3d2bdb' updated_at=2026-04-21T13:37:08.134136+00:00>Ekoa workflows platform strategy
<chat_summary>**Conversation Overview**

Gonçalo, who is building the Ekoa platform, introduced a new sister project called AWC (Agentic Workflow Compositions) — a free, community-editable website where users compose autonomous agent setups by selecting components across a set of defined primitives, then download a zip containing an `instructions.md` file that their chosen CLI platform (initially Claude Code) reads and executes to install the full environment. The project is motivated by Gonçalo's observation that autonomous development platforms like OpenClaw and Hermes are opaque black boxes; AWC aims to make the underlying concepts (heartbeat, classifier, gateway, memory, etc.) transparent, composable, and verifiable. Gonçalo plans to use AWC himself for ongoing workflow creation and eventually open it to the broader community.
```

Doc is up. Now the discussion you asked for. I'm going to push back on a few things and ask the questions that actually matter, in order of how much they unblock subsequent work.

**On Phase 1 — the one I think you're slightly under-scoping**

You said "memory should be working properly" as a single bullet, but I think the memory port is the *biggest* Phase 1 risk. The current `fittings/seed/memory` Fitting is a placeholder that writes a SKILL.md saying "compile memory to markdown." It doesn't *do* anything. The EKOA memory compiler is real code. Porting it isn't a one-line change — it's "throw away the current seed Fitting and replace it with the real EKOA implementation, then verify it actually fires during a gateway-mediated session."

Two questions before I'd touch that:

1. **In EKOA, is the memory compiler a Claude Code hook (`.claude/hooks/*.json` + script), a skill (`.claude/skills/*/SKILL.md`), or both?** Your memory says "obsidian-style memory that actually uses obsidian" but that's the storage backend, not the trigger shape. The trigger shape determines what the Garrison Fitting's `.apm/` layout looks like.

2. **The current gateway runs the SDK in-process — does the EKOA memory compiler hook fire under SDK invocation, or does it only fire under interactive `claude` CLI invocation?** Hooks fire on Claude Code lifecycle events. If the SDK doesn't emit those events, the hook never fires and the memory never compiles, regardless of what we install. This is the kind of thing that fails silently and blows up Phase 2 when you say "what do you remember about me" and nothing comes back.

**On Phase 2 — the project index**

I had it as a `knowledge-base` Fitting, but rethinking: I think it should be a *config field on the Orchestrator* (`projects_root` already is) plus a small **skill** under the `skills` Faculty that exposes "list projects" / "look into project X" as callable tools. Reasoning: knowledge-base is for *static reference content*, but the projects folder is dynamic and the Operative needs to *call* it, not *read from* it. Skills are the right Faculty for callable tools. Want me to switch that in the doc?

**On Phase 3 — the option I'd actually push for**

I lean Option C (SDK sub-agent in same process) but want to flag the real reason against B: spawning a fresh `claude` process means **a fresh context window with no memory of the planning conversation**. You'd have to re-pass the plan as a prompt, which is fine, but you also lose any nuance you built up in the conversation that didn't make it into the plan text. C keeps the orchestrator's full context available to the sub-agent.

But: C requires the SDK to support sub-agents with their own working directories. Need to verify that's a real API before locking in. Want me to verify against the SDK docs as part of Phase 3 prep, or treat that as a Phase 3 implementation-time question?

**On Phase 4 / UI surfaces — the tradeoff worth deciding now**

The doc parks "design richer UI contract Phase 1 vs Phase 4" as an open question, but I actually think this matters earlier than Phase 4. If the Slack Fitting in Phase 1 wants any kind of "show me Slack channel selection / OAuth status" UI, we hit the same limitation. And if memory wants a "browse compiled memory" pane in Phase 1, same thing.

My suggestion: **design the richer UI extension contract in Phase 1, before the Slack Fitting lands.** It's not free, but it's a contract change — once Slack and Memory ship against the v1 contract, retrofitting them is more work than just doing it once.

**Three tactical questions to nail down before next session:**

Doc is updated. Five things changed: Ekus split out from EKOA (Slack/Trello/channels from Ekus, Automations-only from EKOA), Memory rewritten as Claude-Code-native compiler-hook (no port), Slack rewritten as webhook, UI surfaces deferred to Phase 4 with the retrofit cost acknowledged, decision log + parking lot updated.

Now — the answers actually surfaced two new things worth pushing on before we move on.

**1. The SDK hook lifecycle question is now Phase 1's biggest unknown.**

If the gateway runs `@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk` in-process and the SDK doesn't fire the same lifecycle events that interactive `claude` fires, then the `memory-compiler` hook never triggers and Phase 1's Memory work is dead in the water — installed but inert. This isn't speculation; it's a real ambiguity in the SDK's behavior. Three ways to resolve it:

- Read the SDK source / docs to confirm hook event emission.
- Write a tiny smoke test: install a no-op hook that writes to a file when fired, run a turn through the gateway, check the file.
- Ask in the SDK's repo / community.

The smoke test is the cheapest and most decisive. I'd want that done **before** porting the memory-compiler properly, because if the hook doesn't fire we need a different memory strategy (probably: invoke the compiler explicitly from the orchestrator at session-end, as a skill rather than a hook).

**2. The Ekus inventory pass is now more important than I had it.**

You said Trello came from Ekus and Slack will too. Worth taking 20 minutes to look at what *else* is in Ekus that might shortcut Phase 1 or Phase 2 — gateway patterns, channel scaffolding, a soul prompt, a heartbeat shape, project-index code, anything. If Ekus has something usable for the Orchestrator/Soul rewrite or the project index, we save ourselves work.

**Concretely, what I'd do next session (in order):**

1. SDK hook smoke test (10 min, decisive).
2. Ekus inventory pass — what's in there (15-30 min).
3. Based on (1) and (2), draft the actual Phase 1 ticket list with file paths.
4. Then start with the smallest Phase 1 piece — probably the Soul rewrite, since it's pure prompt and unblocks the personality work without needing any Faculty changes.

Logged. Doc is current.

So where we are: Phase 1 is the only phase with no remaining big-ticket unknowns blocking start. Phase 2 has a couple of design questions but they're tractable. Phase 3 has the session-model question and now this observability question, both deferred to when we get there. Phase 4 is far enough out that its open questions don't matter yet.

Two things I want to flag before we go further, since you said keep pushing on details:

**Phase 1 has a sequencing question worth deciding now.**

The Phase 1 scope items aren't naturally serial. You can do them in roughly any order, but the decision on order matters because some of them are blocked by the SDK hook test:

- Soul rewrite — pure prompt work, unblocked, no dependencies.
- Orchestrator rewrite — pure prompt work + a config field, unblocked.
- Trello end-to-end verify — already mostly there, needs a smoke test.
- Slack channel — needs Ekus inventory + tunnel decision, but no hook dependencies.
- Memory port — **blocked by the SDK hook smoke test.** If hooks don't fire, the whole approach changes.

Suggested order: SDK hook smoke test first (decides memory strategy), Ekus inventory next (informs Slack + maybe other shortcuts), then Soul + Orchestrator rewrite in parallel with Slack port, Memory port last when its strategy is clear. Trello verify can slot in anywhere — it's a 10-minute check.

**One thing about the Orchestrator rewrite I want to nail down before drafting it.**

The current placeholder Soul says "direct, transparent, local-first, dogfood-oriented." That's fine for testing. The real Soul needs to encode:

- The PM hat (clarifying questions, scope discipline, refusing to write code in chat when the right answer is to plan)
- The Software Architect hat (system thinking, calling out tradeoffs, pushing back on premature decisions)
- The personal-assistant baseline tone for non-dev work
- How to switch between them — explicit toggle, contextual detection, or both?

The Orchestrator separately needs to encode the *policies* — when to plan, when to spawn, when to ask, when to just answer. Soul = who, Orchestrator = how, per the existing spec.

Question that'll change the whole shape of the Soul rewrite:

