**Roadmap update:** Faculty/shape validator refactor added to parking lot (carried over from your T7 implementation note), Phase 2 implementation observations added to the decision log so the wire-level realities don't get lost (gateway uses `/jobs` not `/chat`, `report_channel` is canonical, validator forces `automations + cli-skill`).

**Phase 3 execution plan:** Six tickets, dependency-ordered. T1 (`for_consumers`) ships first because it's small and isolated. T2 (UI contract v2) is the keystone — everything visual depends on it. T3 (Artifact Store) and T4 (Documents) layer on top. T5 (chat linking) is the smallest and ties the cross-Fitting story together. T6 is verification.

When you're back to copying-and-pasting between here and Claude Code, **T1 is the next standalone brief to draft.** It's the smallest, most isolated ticket — a good warm-up after a phase change. T2 is bigger and may want more thought on the validator refactor question first.

If you find yourself slowed down at T4, this is the likely reason — and it'd be cleaner to handle as T0 (before T1) than retroactively at T4. If you want, I can rewrite the plan to put the validator refactor at T0.

**2. The render shape for `for_consumers` injection (T1) is proposed, not decided.** Markdown indentation, bold provider lines, fallback-to-summary behavior — all reasonable defaults but worth a glance before T1 ships. If Claude Code finds the existing assembly logic outputs a different shape, follow that shape rather than my proposal.

**3. T2 is the ticket where Garrison's UI grows for the first time since Phase 1.** That means the existing frontend may have shortcuts and assumptions that contract v2 needs to navigate around. Especially the sidebar nav structure — if it's hardcoded today, contract v2 needs to make it dynamic. The brief flags this in T2's open questions; worth Claude Code reading the frontend code first before drafting.

### assistant — 2026-05-08 17:12 UTC

 Solid. Big phase landed clean — 133/133 green, every ticket coherent with the spec, and the implementation surfaced three real-world adaptations worth recording before they fade:

**1. Static registry of view components instead of dynamic import.** Pragmatic. Dynamic-import-from-Fitting-entry-file is the more flexible long-term shape but adds bundler complexity. A static registry says "Garrison knows about its seed Fittings' views at build time," which is fine for v1 where every Fitting in play is a seed. When third-party Fittings start shipping their own views, the registry gets revisited.

**2. `cli-skill` shape now valid under `knowledge-base` Faculty.** This is the exact Faculty/shape validator question I parked in T0 of the Phase 3 plan, resolved minimally rather than refactored fully. That's the right call — solve the immediate need, leave the broader refactor for when more Fittings hit the same wall. The parking lot entry stays accurate.

**3. Edit view is textarea, not tiptap.** Pragmatic too. tiptap-on-markdown is genuinely fiddly for v1; plain textarea does the job for "I want to edit a doc the agent created" and the upgrade path is clear. Documenting the decision rather than hiding it is exactly right.

Roadmap updated. Three phases done, four to go.

**Where you are now:**

- ✅ Phase 1 — seed Operative shape (DONE)
- ✅ Phase 2 — PA functionality (DONE — runtime DoD on T6/T7 still your homework)
- ✅ Phase 3 — Documents + Artifact Store + UI v2 (DONE)

- Phase 6 — Automations
- Phase 7 — Tasks Faculty

**Phase 4 is the next meaningful chunk.** It's the phase where the conversational Operative starts *doing* coding work, not just discussing it. The roadmap has it sized as: claude-code-planning-tool integration, plan approval flow, then execution via SDK sub-agent (Option C with B as fallback) in the right project folder. Phase 2's projects-index Fitting feeds the project resolution; Phase 3's Documents Fitting captures the plan; Phase 4 is the missing piece that closes the loop from "let's discuss feature X" to "feature X is implemented."

A few open questions in Phase 4 that deferred from earlier sessions:

1. **Session model — A vs B vs C** (same gateway session / spawn fresh / SDK sub-agent). Roadmap leans C with B as fallback.
2. **Observability of spawned work** — chat summary, second log stream, or full second session view. Deferred to Phase 4 itself.
3. **Planning tool source** — built-in Claude Code or own skill.

You said "you decide what to do next" so: **draft the Phase 4 execution plan now**, ready for when you're back at the keyboard. Same shape as Phase 1/2/3 — ordered tickets, dependency graph, scope/done/out-of-scope/open-questions per ticket. Should be smaller than Phase 3 since there's no UI work — Phase 4 is mostly orchestration plumbing.

Both staged.

**Roadmap:** Phase 3 marked done with commit references and the three implementation adaptations recorded. Parking lot grew three Phase 3 retrospective items (textarea→tiptap upgrade path, mini-renderer→react-markdown, static→dynamic view registry).

**Phase 4 plan:** Seven tickets, with the four roadmap open questions pre-decided so the plan is decisive instead of forking on every page. Headline calls:

- **Session model: Option C** (SDK sub-agent), with B as runtime fallback per execution if the spike (T1) reveals limits.
- **Planning tool: own skill**, not the built-in. Wraps Claude Code's planning behavior in a structure that supports our approval flow.
- **Observability: second log stream in the Run tab.** Not a full session view — that's still Workspace territory, deferred.
- **Approval: text-reply in chat with the plan rendered as markdown** plus a Documents link the user can edit before approving. No Slack approval, no buttons, no structured UI.
- **Mid-execution: kill and restart**, not message injection.

These are calls. If you disagree with any of them, push back before starting T1 — they're decisions baked into every subsequent ticket.

**T1 is genuinely a spike, not a normal ticket.** It produces a one-page report that confirms (or invalidates) the entire Phase 4 design. The plan is honest about this: if the spike fails, T2 retargets to Option B. Don't skip T1.

The standalone Claude Code brief for T1 would be small and self-contained. Want me to draft it now, or wait until you're ready to start the phase?

Roadmap updated. Phase 4 marked done with the three implementation observations captured.

**Where you are now:**

- ✅ Phase 1 — Seed Operative shape
- ✅ Phase 2 — PA functionality
- ✅ Phase 3 — Documents + Artifact Store + UI v2
- ✅ Phase 4 — Plan-then-execute on real projects
- ⏭️ **Phase 5 — Trenches** (next)
- Phase 6 — Automations
- Phase 7 — Tasks Faculty

You're past the conversational + foundational phases. From here, each phase is more self-contained:

- **Phase 5 (Trenches)** — terminal, screen sharing, Tailscale-host launchers. Lifts working code from Harmonika rather than designing new primitives. The roadmap pre-agreed an analysis session at the start of this phase to validate the port plan against live Harmonika code.
- **Phase 6 (Automations)** — port from EKOA. Choose between `cortex/` (Playwright API) and `automato/` (raw CDP) before any porting work.
- **Phase 7 (Tasks Faculty)** — first-party Kanban-as-control-plane, replaces Trello as source of truth.

A natural decision point: **do you want to continue strict roadmap order, or skip ahead?** Reasons you might skip:

- **Phase 5 is the most "for me, the technical user" phase.** If you're not yet feeling the lack of in-Garrison terminal + screen sharing, it's deferrable.
- **Phase 7 (Tasks Faculty) is genuinely transformative.** It's the foundation for the autonomous workflow vision. Doing Phase 7 next means the heartbeat-pickup pattern lands sooner. Phase 4's coding-subagent is already there to handle tier-3+ tasks the heartbeat picks up.
- **Phase 6 is the largest port.** Two automation engines to choose between, full UI surface, retention concerns. It's the most external-dependency-heavy phase.

If I had to pick the highest-leverage next phase: **Phase 7**. It activates the autonomous-workflow dimension your roadmap's North Star item 10 is about. The pieces are all in place — Memory remembers tasks across sessions, Orchestrator routes work via Classifier, Documents captures plans, coding-subagent executes them. Phase 7 is what makes this a *system* rather than a collection of capabilities.

Phase 5 (Trenches) is more of a quality-of-life upgrade for you specifically. Phase 6 (Automations) is foundational but its dependencies are cleaner with Phase 7 in place (Tasks can drive automation runs).

You've been on autopilot saying "you decide" — for this one I'd recommend asking explicitly:

Phase 5 plan staged. Seven tickets, T0 is the analysis session that the roadmap pre-agreed.

**Three things that make Phase 5 different from prior phases:**

**1. T0 is genuinely load-bearing.** Phase 1's investigation prompt told Claude Code to explore the EKOA/Ekus codebases and report findings. This is the same shape but more decisive — it has to *settle* questions, not just enumerate them. The output is a committed `PHASE_5_ANALYSIS.md` that subsequent tickets reference. You'll likely run T0 as a separate Claude Code session before drafting any of the T1+ briefs, so the analysis can inform those briefs.

**2. The Trenches code is *Garrison core*, not Fittings.** Most of what we've shipped lives in `fittings/seed/`. Trenches lives in `src/trenches/` (or wherever the frontend conventions put it). That's a precedent — the roadmap notes Trenches *could eventually* be a Faculty surface, but isn't yet. So this is the first phase that adds substantial functionality to Garrison-core rather than as a Fitting. Worth being explicit about.

**3. The "two sessions of the same Operative" nuance is real.** I called this out in cross-phase notes and again in T3's banner injection. When you click Open Orchestrator, you get a terminal session running Claude Code with the assembled system prompt — that's *not* the same session as the chat tab. They share compiled memory but not turn history. Users will be confused by this; the banner in T3 is the v1 mitigation. Worth reading T3's scope to make sure you're happy with how this lands.

Standing by for "T0 done" when you've run the analysis. Then I can draft T1's standalone brief informed by the real Harmonika lift inventory.

### assistant — 2026-05-10 09:27 UTC

 This is a clean architectural simplification. You're right that "Trenches as a separate area" was a category error — the platform thesis is "Faculties + Fittings compose," and the moment we build a separate area we're saying "but actually some things don't follow the rules." That breaks the principle.

Reading your document carefully, what you're describing is genuinely simpler than the current Phase 5 plan, with three real wins:

1. **No new architectural concept.** Trenches needed top-level navigation, code separation, special-cased UI integration. Armory needs none of that — it's the existing Faculty/Fitting machinery applied to UI tooling.

2. **The Sequoias decomposition is the verification milestone in itself.** You replace it with three Armory Fittings; if they work, the pattern is proven. That's a much cleaner success criterion than "did we build a new area correctly."

3. **The Operative bridge is design-now-cost-zero.** Action declarations using `provides`/`consumes` mean the same wiring carries Operative-invokable tool actions. Trenches's "Open Orchestrator" was awkward precisely because it sat outside the wiring graph; in Armory, that's just an action declaration on a Fitting.

Three things I want to push on before rewriting the roadmap, because they shape the rewrite:

**1. Naming.** "Armory" pairs with Operatives well and fits the Garrison metaphor. But "Armory" is *singular* in your document — implying a single Faculty named `armory` containing many tool kinds. The more I read, though, the more it looks like you want a *family* of Faculties (`worktree-management`, `session-view`, `terminal`, `screen-share`, `browser`), each with its own contract. So: is "Armory" the *family name* (a category in the shell that groups multiple faculties), or is it *one* Faculty whose Fittings happen to all be tools?

I think you mean the former — multiple Faculties grouped under an Armory section in the shell. That makes sense; it lets `terminal` and `worktree-management` have separate contracts while still living in the same area. Worth being explicit because the rewrite differs depending on the answer.

**2. The Sequoias decomposition into three Faculties is interesting but worth questioning the cardinality.** Your document has `worktree-management`, `session-view`, and `terminal` as three Faculties. But `session-view` is genuinely generic — sessions exist for terminals, for sub-agents, for whatever. Calling it an Armory Faculty implies it's only for the Armory, which I don't think you mean. Should `session-view` be a cross-Faculty primitive (like memory was) that the Armory's `terminal` and `worktree-management` *consume*, rather than its own Armory Faculty?

Possible answer: yes, separate it. Session monitoring is a generic capability; it lives in its own Faculty (maybe `sessions`?) and the Armory Fittings consume it.

**3. What replaces Phase 5's "Open Orchestrator" / "Open Claude Code" buttons?** They're not Sequoias-derived; they were Trenches-specific. In the new model, are they:
   - A small Fitting under the `terminal` Faculty that ships specialized launch commands?
   - Actions on the `terminal` Fitting itself?
   - Dropped from this phase, picked up later?

I'd lean: actions on the `terminal` Fitting. They're "spawn a terminal with these specific defaults" — a natural action of a terminal Fitting, not a separate Fitting.

Two questions for you, then I'll do the rewrite:


Both files staged.

**Roadmap changes:**

- Phase 5 fully rewritten as Armory (family of Faculties: `worktree-management`, `session-view`, `terminal`, `screen-share`, `browser`-deferred). Sequoias decomposition is the verification milestone.
- North star item 8 updated.
- Cross-cutting context: Sequoias added as primary Phase 5 reference; Harmonika downgraded to secondary.
- UI surfaces section: "Trenches sidesteps the contract" replaced with "Armory uses contract v2 fully."
- New decision log entry capturing the reframe with the reasoning ("category error against the platform thesis").
- Old "UI extension contract stays per-tab-single-pane through Phase 5" decision annotated with a 2026-05-08 supersession note.
- Parking lot updated: removed obsolete "Trenches as a Faculty surface" entry (that's literally what Armory is now), reworded other Phase 5 entries.

**Phase 5 execution plan rewritten:** Ten tickets (T0-T9). T0 is still the analysis session, but with more to settle. T1 is shell plumbing for Faculty-family rendering. T2 is the foundational `terminal` Fitting. T3 + T4 are the Sequoias-specific worktree-management and session-view Fittings. T5 + T6 layer features onto T2. T7 is screen-share. **T8 is the actual phase milestone — Sequoias retirement.** T9 is the verification document.

Three things worth flagging before T0 starts:

**1. T0 has more to settle now.** APM gap-find is the biggest unknown. If contract v2 doesn't support multi-Fitting Faculties or Fitting-to-Fitting action invocation cleanly, the gap-fix work might be larger than any individual T2-T9 ticket. T1 is split-noted (T1a gap-fix, T1b shell area) precisely because of this.

**2. The "Faculty family" mechanism is a real platform addition.** Up to now, Faculties were mostly a UX taxonomy with the wiring living in capability kinds. Phase 5 makes Faculty *grouping* matter at the platform level — the shell needs to know which Faculties belong to the Armory family. T0 picks the mechanism (declarative field vs static registry vs convention).

**3. T8 (Sequoias retirement) is unusual as a ticket.** It's not implementation — it's an attestation that the work is done well enough that you actually use Armory instead of Sequoias for 3+ days. That's the only meaningful test of "did we replicate Sequoias's value."

Standing by for "T0 done" or whatever's next.

### assistant — 2026-05-11 07:58 UTC

 Let me make sure I've understood what you're describing, then push on the architecture, then answer your phase-numbering question.

---

**The use case, restated to confirm:**

- You have N Macs on Tailscale (N=3 today). One is "the host" — always-on, runs Garrison, runs the Operative.
- You want to work *from* any machine. When you open Garrison's UI on Mac 2, you're pointing it at Mac 1's Garrison instance. Same Operative, same memory, same compositions.
- When you create a worktree "on the machine I'm working on," it's actually created on Mac 2 — but Mac 1's Garrison is orchestrating it. You sit in front of Mac 2; the worktree, the terminal session, the Claude Code process live there; the *management* lives on Mac 1.
- Same shape for the Operative's own actions: when it needs to run a command somewhere, it should be able to choose which machine. The Obsidian vault sync is one example — Mac 1 reads/writes the vault on Mac 1's disk, but other machines need it too.

Three things you want to confirm or push on:

1. **Whether SSH + Mosh alone is enough**, or whether we need a "local bridge" agent on each remote Mac.
2. **Whether to extend this to the Operative's own actions** (not just the user's worktree workflow), or scope down to just worktrees + Claude Code sessions in v1.
3. **Phase numbering** — is this Phase 5.5, a new Phase 6, or part of Phase 5?

---

**My read on each:**

**1. SSH alone is not enough. A local bridge is the right design.**

SSH covers "run a command on the other machine and capture output." That gets you basic terminal spawning, basic file operations via stdin/stdout. What it doesn't cover well:

- **Long-lived stateful connections.** A Sequoias-style session view needs continuous status updates from N remote machines. SSH connections per worktree per machine get expensive fast, and reconnect storms when network blips happen are a real problem.
- **Filesystem watching.** "Tell me when this file changes on Mac 2" via SSH means polling or `fswatch` over SSH — fragile and slow.
- **Reverse calls.** A worktree process on Mac 2 finishes a long task and needs to notify Mac 1's Garrison. SSH is unidirectional in the way we'd use it; reverse needs another channel.
- **Bidirectional streaming with consistent framing.** Terminal output, file changes, process events all want a single channel with typed messages. SSH gives you a byte stream; you'd reinvent the framing.
- **Auth and lifecycle.** SSH-per-feature means SSH-key-per-feature. A local bridge means one authenticated connection per machine, multiplexed.

What you actually want is the bridge model Ekoa is building toward. One small process on each remote Mac that:

- Connects out to Mac 1 (the host) over Tailscale.
- Holds a persistent WebSocket (or gRPC, or whatever — pick one).
- Exposes RPC-like operations: spawn-process, watch-file, list-worktrees, run-command, etc.
- Surfaces events back to Mac 1: process finished, file changed, output line, status changed.

Mac 1's Garrison treats this as a **uniform interface to "do something on Machine X"** without caring which machine. The bridge is small and dumb; the orchestration logic stays on Mac 1.

This isn't more complex than the SSH approach once you've built more than two things — it's *less* complex because every new "I want to do X on remote machine" reuses the same bridge instead of inventing another SSH command.

**2. On scope: yes to "Operative actions on remote machines," but with a constraint.**

The way you described it has the right shape. Mac 1's Operative is the single brain. When it decides "run this command," it picks which machine to run it on. The bridge is the transport.

The constraint: **don't put the Operative *itself* on remote machines** for v1. One Operative, one Mac 1, one source of truth. The bridge gives Mac 1 *hands* on the other machines, not *brains*. The "subset of Operative on each machine" idea you floated would get complicated fast — split memory, split routing decisions, conflict resolution between Operative instances. Not v1.

For the Obsidian vault sync: yes, the bridge is the right home for this. The bridge on each machine can keep a directory in sync with Mac 1's authoritative copy, push local changes upstream, pull remote changes down. It's a small generic capability the bridge already needs to have ("watch a directory, mirror it to the host") — Obsidian is just the first user.

**3. Phase numbering: this is Phase 6.**

Reasons it's not Phase 5.5 / part of Phase 5:

- **It's a real Faculty addition**, not a refinement of Armory. Armory already shipped (in the plan) with single-machine local hosts plus simple SSH wrappers for Tailscale launches. The bridge is a separate, larger concept — its own Faculty, its own contracts, its own protocol design.
- **It depends on Armory being shipped.** The worktree-management Fitting, the terminal Fitting, the session-view Fitting all need to gain "and on machine X" awareness via the bridge. Building bridge work *alongside* Armory means doing both at once and risking neither solid.
- **It's the kind of thing that benefits from Armory being real first.** You'll have concrete pain — "I want this worktree on Mac 2, but Garrison only knows Mac 1" — that informs the bridge design. Building the bridge speculatively before feeling the pain risks overengineering.

Reasons it's not "new Phase 6, push Automations and Tasks back":

- Automations and Tasks (current Phase 6 and 7) are independent of the bridge. They don't gain anything from the bridge landing first.
- Calling it Phase 6 doesn't push anything back; it just sequences this work where it naturally fits.

**My recommendation: insert as Phase 6, bump Automations → 7 and Tasks → 8.**

This means: Phase 5 (Armory, local-first) → Phase 6 (Bridge, multi-machine) → Phase 7 (Automations) → Phase 8 (Tasks).

The bridge phase is genuinely "Phase 5 plus one capability the platform doesn't have yet." That capability is significant enough to deserve its own phase.

---

**On naming the Faculty:**

You floated "multi-machine," "multi-device," "multi-environment." None quite right.

- **multi-machine** is descriptive but flat.
- **multi-device** suggests phones/tablets, which isn't this.
- **multi-environment** is too abstract.

A few options I'd consider:

- **Outposts** — fits the Garrison metaphor (garrison + outposts), suggests "places where the Garrison's reach extends." Each remote Mac is an outpost; the bridge is what makes a Mac into an outpost. Single-word noun.
- **Frontier** — suggests reach beyond the home base. Less precise about "machines specifically."
- **Reach** — verb-ish, generic.

Lean: **Outposts**. The Faculty kind would be `outpost` (singular per remote machine). A Fitting under that Faculty represents one remote machine.

So the architecture would be: an `outposts` Faculty (cardinality `many` — you can have multiple outposts), each Outpost Fitting connects to one bridge running on a specific Mac. The Armory's worktree-management, terminal, and session-view Fittings *consume* the outposts capability and gain a host selector.

---

**Three questions to lock this down before I rewrite the roadmap:**

_[tool_use: `ask_user_input_v0`]_


The bridge phase plan will look different from prior phases — it has real protocol design questions T0 needs to answer (WebSocket vs gRPC, message framing, auth model, reconnect strategy), and it has a "what does the bridge install look like on a fresh Mac" angle similar to the memory-compiler clone-from-repo pattern.

```json
{"description": "Insert new Phase 6 Outposts before Phase 7 Automations", "new_str": "---\n\n## Phase 6 — Outposts: multi-machine reach via a local bridge\n\n**Outcome:** Garrison gains an `outposts` Faculty. Each Outpost\nFitting represents one remote Mac (or other host) running a small\n**bridge** process. From a single Operative on the host machine,\nGarrison can spawn worktrees, run terminals, execute commands,\nwatch files, and (over time) carry the full Operative action\nsurface across N machines connected via Tailscale.\n\nThe phase is the foundation for \"I sit at any Mac; my Garrison\nruns on the always-on Mac; everything I do feels local.\"\n\n### Why this phase, why now\n\nThe user runs three Macs on Tailscale. Today Garrison is\nsingle-machine: one Operative on one host, with everything (Trello,\nCalendar, Slack, Memory, Documents, Artifacts, Armory) bound to\nthat machine's filesystem and processes. To use Garrison from a\nsecond Mac, the user has to either remote-desktop into the first\nMac or run a second Garrison instance — neither composes with the\nsingle-Operative model.\n\nPhase 5 (Armory) shipped Sequoias-decomposed Fittings that handle\nworktrees + sessions + … [truncated]
```

Both files staged.

**Roadmap changes from the prior turn:**

- Phase 6 (Outposts) inserted between Phase 5 (Armory) and old Phase 6.
- Automations bumped to Phase 7. Tasks bumped to Phase 8.
- North star gained item 9 (Outposts); old 9 and 10 renumbered to 10 and 11.
- Cross-cutting context notes that Sequoias's multi-machine support is what Phase 6 restores as a proper Faculty.
- Decision log entry recording the Outposts addition with the key shape (one Operative on the host, bridges give it hands not brains, JSON-over-WebSocket-over-Tailscale, etc.).
- Parking lot gained Phase 6 entries (protocol choice, auth details, bridge auto-update, FS path semantics, multiplexing, failure UI, vault-sync direction).

**Phase 6 execution plan:** Ten tickets (T0-T9). T0 is protocol design — this phase needs settled wire format before any code is written. T1 creates the bridge as its own repo (memory-compiler precedent). T2 is host-side Fitting. T3 is bootstrap flow with a one-liner script. T4-T6 retrofit the three Armory Fittings with outpost-awareness. T7 is the Operative-side `outpost-actions` skill. T8 is vault-sync as the first non-Armory consumer. T9 is verification.

Three things worth flagging before T0 starts:

**1. T0 is design, not exploration.** Unlike Phase 5's T0 which was "go read Sequoias and Harmonika," Phase 6's T0 is "commit to a protocol shape." The roadmap leans WebSocket+JSON; T0's job is to make that concrete enough that T1 and T2 can implement independently against the same spec.

**2. The host-side WebSocket-server question is real.** T2 flags it — the gateway currently does HTTP, and the Outposts Fittings need a long-lived WebSocket server. Adding a WebSocket route to the gateway is small mechanically, but the *subscription model* (how do Fittings receive events from connections they didn't open?) needs designing. Worth checking how the gateway is currently structured before T1 ships.

**3. The vault-sync Fitting is unidirectional v1.** If you want bidirectional, that's a real chunk more work — it's not Phase 6 scope. T8 is intentionally minimal so the bridge proves itself as the substrate for sync without sync itself being the focus.

Standing by for "T0 done" or whatever's next.

### assistant — 2026-05-11 09:01 UTC

 Three options on the table. Let me give you my honest read on each.

---

**Option A: Clone Garrison onto the development machine.**

Fastest path to "working today." Takes 10-15 minutes — clone, `apm install`, set up Slack/Trello/Calendar tokens (or skip them, you have them on automation), boot. You'd have two Garrison instances: one on automation, one on development. Independent Operatives, independent memory, independent worktrees.

**Real cost:** memory divergence. Your compiled memory lives on the automation machine in `~/Projects/ekus/obsidian-vault/Compiled/`. The development-machine Operative starts with empty memory unless you also clone or sync the vault. You'd be working with an Operative that doesn't know what you've discussed before. By tonight your two Operatives would have diverged memory. Reconciling that later is annoying.

This is the "second Garrison instance" scenario your Phase 6 reframe was specifically about *not* wanting.

---

**Option B: Just work on the development machine without Garrison, plain Claude Code.**

You don't actually need Garrison to write Ekoa code today. Plain `claude` in a terminal works fine. You'd lose: heartbeat suggestions, Trello-aware briefings, the Operative wrapping. You'd keep: actual code-writing productivity.

**Honest read:** you've been building Garrison to be useful to you. If "useful to me" means "I open my development machine and Claude Code is enough," that's a real signal about which Phase 6 features matter most. But it also means today's work doesn't move Garrison forward, and you mentioned wanting to fit Garrison work in.

---

**Option C: Execute Phase 6 today, use it for real on Ekoa work this evening.**

You said you think this is doable in under an hour. I'll be honest: I disagree with that estimate, but not by as much as you might expect, and the gap is informative.

Looking at the Phase 6 plan I drafted — T0 through T9 — most of it is genuinely small. T0 (protocol design) is real thinking but the document is committable in 30-45 minutes. T1 (bridge daemon) is a few hundred lines of Node. T2 (host Fitting) is comparable. T3 (bootstrap) is a shell script. T4 (terminal on outpost) is one Fitting update. That's the path-to-useful — those five tickets, not all nine.

**T5, T6, T7, T8 are not needed for tonight.** You don't need worktree-management routed through outposts to start using outposts for *terminals*. You can `cd` into your existing development-machine repo manually in an SSH'd terminal on the outpost, run `claude`, work normally. Worktree-management-on-outpost is a quality-of-life win, not a blocker.

**Realistic estimate for the path-to-useful (T0-T4 only):** 3-5 hours. Not one. Reasons:
- T0 protocol design has subtleties that look obvious but bite during implementation (heartbeat semantics, reconnect state recovery, error propagation through async streaming). 45 minutes is reasonable for the doc; longer if you want it solid.
- T1 bridge: writing the daemon is fast, but the first end-to-end test (auth → spawn process → receive output events → kill) is where bugs hide. Plan for 60-90 min including debugging.
- T2 host Fitting: the WebSocket-server-in-the-gateway question I flagged earlier is real work. The gateway doesn't currently host WebSockets. Adding that route + subscription model is maybe 60-90 min.
- T3 bootstrap: 30 min. Easy.
- T4 terminal-on-outpost: 60-90 min. Most of the time is the Armory terminal Fitting's PTY layer routing through the bridge protocol instead of local node-pty.

That's 4-6 hours total. If Claude Code is fast and the protocol design holds up under implementation, maybe 3.5. If anything bites, 6+. Probably you'd be done by end of day, but not "in an hour."

**The honest question for option C:** is the goal to have outposts working tonight, or to have outposts working *in time for tonight's Ekoa work*? If you start now and it takes 5 hours, your Ekoa work happens through it. If it takes 8, you're working manually anyway.

---

**My recommendation: a hybrid.**

Run T0 + T1 + T2 + T3 + T4 today as a focused chunk. Skip T5-T8 for now. They land later this week or next.

The minimum-viable Phase 6 is: bridge connects, terminal-on-outpost works. That's enough to do Ekoa development today *through* outposts — you SSH-equivalently to your development machine via the bridge, you `cd` to the repo manually, you `claude` and work. The Operative on the automation machine can also call `run_on(development-machine, "...")` for status checks, but you can build the `outpost-actions` skill (T7) tomorrow without that blocking tonight.

What you give up vs full Phase 6:
- No worktree-management routed through outpost (you make worktrees manually with `git worktree add` in the SSH'd terminal)
- No session-view aggregation (you watch your terminal directly)
- No Operative-side `run_on` (the Operative stays automation-machine-local for tool calls; you reach development from the terminal yourself)
- No vault sync (you live with stale vault on development for now, or set up a separate `rsync` cron)

What you get:
- Garrison on automation orchestrates terminals on development through the Armory UI
- All your existing Operative, memory, briefings, Trello integration stay on automation, intact
- Real-world stress-test of the bridge protocol today, on Ekoa work that matters
- The remaining Phase 6 tickets become "what tomorrow improves" rather than "what blocks tonight"

If T0-T4 takes longer than 4 hours, you can fall back to **Option A** (clone Garrison to development) as the escape hatch. You'd be ~3 hours in by then and have learned a lot about the protocol — that's not wasted; it informs the rest of Phase 6 next session.

**My single-sentence recommendation:** start T0 now, set a hard 90-minute timer; if the protocol doc doesn't feel solid by then, fall back to Option A (clone Garrison) and ship Phase 6 properly next session.

A few things I want to flag before you paste it in:

**1. The "stop and fall back" instruction at the bottom is real.** If the worked example in §3.10 doesn't write cleanly, that's a design hole, not a writing-skill problem. The brief tells Claude Code to halt and report rather than push through. That matters for your 90-minute window — better to know at minute 80 that the design has a hole and pivot to Option A than to ship a half-baked spec.

**2. The operation list is intentionally tight.** I considered including `connection.reconnected` as a first-class event, but cut it — `connection.heartbeat` resuming after a gap is enough signal. If you find during T1/T2 that you need explicit reconnect events, add them in v2.

**3. The brief deliberately doesn't ask for diagrams.** Just JSON examples and the worked trace. Diagrams take time and add little value for a v1 protocol; ASCII trace in §3.10 is more useful than boxes-and-arrows.

**4. T0 is the only ticket today that's *all* design.** T1 (bridge daemon) and beyond will reference this document; if T0 ships well, the rest of today's work has a stable foundation. If T0 stalls, the rest doesn't happen well today regardless of effort.

```
    21	3. Knows where my projects live and can read them for context without
    22	   me pasting GitHub sources.
    23	4. Remembers things across sessions via the Memory Faculty (the
    24	   user's existing Obsidian-backed memory-compiler with Claude Code
    25	   hooks).
    26	5. Looks at its task list on its heartbeat, suggests what to work
    27	   on, and — once I trust it — does autonomous tier-1/2 work.
    28	   Tasks live in a first-party Tasks Faculty with a Kanban UI; the
    29	   board doubles as the visible control plane for autonomous work.
    30	   Trello (and other external trackers) sync optionally.
    31	6. Builds documents alongside conversations the way claude.ai does
    32	   — the PM/Architect hat captures decisions and plans into a
    33	   browsable, editable markdown corpus without me asking. All
    34	   artifacts the Operative produces (documents now, automation
    35	   videos and voice audio later) live in a unified Artifact Store
    36	   browsable from one place.
    37	7. Plans features via Claude Code's planning tool, asks me to approve,
    38	   then executes them in the right project folder, in the right
    39	   session shape.
    40	8. Gives me a tooling surface (the Armory) where worktrees,
    41	   sessions, terminals, screen sharing, and other tools compose
    42	   the same way agentic primitives do — Faculties and Fittings,
    43	   wired by provides/consumes. The Sequoias worktree-manager
    44	   becomes three Armory Fittings rather than a separate app.
    45	9. Runs and self-improves browser automations (Playwright-based, ported
    46	   from EKOA) with a UI surface where I can fix, replay, and feedback.
    47	10. Treats the Kanban board a
… [truncated]
```

```json
{"command": "python3 << 'EOF'\nimport re\n\nwith open('/home/claude/GARRISON_ROADMAP.md', 'r') as f:\n    content = f.read()\n\n# Phase 7 (Tasks) → Phase 8\n# Phase 6 (Automations) → Phase 7\n# Phase 5 (Workbench) → stays\n# Phases 1-4 → stay\n\n# Replace highest first\ncontent = re.sub(r'\\bPhase 7\\b', 'Phase ZZEIGHT', content)\ncontent = re.sub(r'\\bPhase 6\\b', 'Phase ZZSEVEN', content)\n\ncontent = content.replace('Phase ZZEIGHT', 'Phase 8')\ncontent = content.replace('Phase ZZSEVEN', 'Phase 7')\n\n# Hyphenated forms\ncontent = re.sub(r'\\bPhase-7-', 'Phase-ZZEIGHT-', content)\ncontent = re.sub(r'\\bPhase-6-', 'Phase-ZZSEVEN-', content)\ncontent = content.replace('Phase-ZZEIGHT-', 'Phase-8-')\ncontent = content.replace('Phase-ZZSEVEN-', 'Phase-7-')\n\n# Parenthetical forms\ncontent = re.sub(r'\\(Phase 7\\)', '(Phase ZZEIGHT)', content)\ncontent = re.sub(r'\\(Phase 6\\)', '(Phase ZZSEVEN)', content)\ncontent = content.replace('(Phase ZZEIGHT)', '(Phase 8)')\ncontent = content.replace('(Phase ZZSEVEN)', '(Phase 7)')\n\nwith open('/home/claude/GARRISON_ROADMAP.md', 'w') as f:\n    f.write(content)\nprint(\"done\")\nEOF", "description": "Bump inline references"}
```

```json
{"description": "Insert Phase 6 Outposts before Phase 7 Automations", "new_str": "---\n\n## Phase 6 — Outposts: multi-machine reach via a local bridge\n\n**Outcome:** Garrison gains an `outposts` Faculty. Each Outpost\nFitting represents one remote Mac (or other host) running a small\n**bridge** process. From a single Operative on the host machine,\nGarrison can spawn worktrees, run terminals, execute commands,\nwatch files, and (over time) carry the full Operative action\nsurface across N machines connected via Tailscale.\n\nThe phase is the foundation for \"I sit at any Mac; my Garrison\nruns on the always-on Mac; everything I do feels local.\"\n\n### Why this phase, why now\n\nThe user runs three Macs on Tailscale: the **automation machine**\n(Mac Mini M4, 16 GB, always on — hosts Garrison + Operative), the\n**development machine** (MacBook Pro M1 Max, 32 GB, always on —\nprimary code work), and the **portable machine** (MacBook Air M4,\n16 GB, intermittent uptime — carried around).\n\nToday Garrison is single-machine: one Operative on one host, with\neverything (Trello, Calendar, Slack, Memory, Documents, Artifacts,\nWorkbench) bound to that machine's filesystem and processes. … [truncated]
```

**Base:** Took the codebase version as authoritative — it has the real Phase 5 implementation observations (Workbench shipped, 4 Faculties, naming-collision resolution, Phase 5.5 same-day landing).

**Renaming applied throughout:** "Armory" → "Workbench" in all references to the tool area, while preserving:
- The naming note that documents the collision resolution
- The decision log entries that record the rename's history
- Genuine references to "Armory" as the Fitting registry browser at `/armory`

**Phase 6 (Outposts) inserted:** Full section between Phase 5 and old Phase 6. Adapted to use your real machine names (automation/development/portable) and to reference the codebase's "Workbench" naming throughout. Mentions integration with the actually-shipped Workbench Fittings (4 Faculties, no browser).

**Phases renumbered:** Automations 6 → 7, Tasks 7 → 8. All inline references updated (decision log, parking lot, cross-cutting context, UI surfaces section).

**North Star updated:** Item 8 says "Workbench" (was "Armory" in the codebase version — they hadn't updated it). New item 9 for Outposts. Old 9/10 became 10/11.

**Decision log:** Added a 2026-05-11 entry for Phase 6 Outposts addition, right after the Phase 5 implementation entry.

**Parking lot:** Updated Phase 5 entries to use Workbench naming and to note where impl resolved them. Added 7 Phase 6 entries for Outposts. Fixed stale Phase 8 references to Phase 7 for Automations.

**Cross-cutting context:** Sequoias reference updated to mention Phase 6 Outposts restoring multi-machine support. Added the three-Mac context (automation/development/portable with specs) as settled context so future briefs reference it.

**On your workflow suggestion** — "always copy the most current roadmap to the codebase and the document to execute and then ask it to execute it" — this is exactly the right pattern. Two things make it work well:

1. **Treat the roadmap as the canonical source of truth** for the codebase. When implementation observations land, they go in the decision log there. Future planning sessions start from that file, not from any older version in chat history.
2. **Execution plans are derivatives.** They get drafted per phase from the roadmap, handed to Claude Code, and discarded after the phase ships. The roadmap captures what we learned; the execution plan is scaffolding.

The merge I just did is the kind of thing that's slightly painful but rarely needed. As long as the codebase roadmap is the canonical version and gets updated every session, future merges should be small or unnecessary. Ready for T0 whenever you are.

### assistant — 2026-05-11 10:20 UTC

 Solid T0. Three things stand out:

**1. The three additions are real maturity signals, not scope creep.** Each was *forced by existing consumer code*, not invented:
- `process.resize` — PTY terminals genuinely require it; without it, ssh'd terminals would have broken column counts and wrong wrap behavior.
- `handle` instead of `pid` — this is the better design even for non-correlation reasons (PIDs are reused by the OS), and the fact that trenches already does this means T1's bridge implementation has a working pattern to match.
- `base_branch`, `repo_path`, `ports`, `env_files` — comes from the actual Sequoias worktree metadata file. Discovering this in T0 saves a discover-during-T5 surprise that would have meant a protocol bump.

This is exactly what T0 was supposed to catch. The fact that all three came from reading existing consumer code (rather than from speculation) means the protocol is grounded.

**2. The `fs.watch + fs.read` pattern for session status is the right call.** Adding a `session.changed` event type would have leaked Sequoias's specific data model into the bridge protocol. Keeping the bridge dumb (generic fs.watch) and letting the host-side consumer interpret the file is the better layering. Same shape as how the Operating System gives you `inotify` and you decide what file changes mean.

**3. File placement at `docs/phases/PHASE_6_PROTOCOL.md` is right.** The brief said repo root because I didn't know about your existing `docs/phases/` convention. Following the existing convention is correct.

**The note about the uncommitted GARRISON_ROADMAP.md changes is important.** Those are my merged changes from the prior turn (Phase 6 inserted, Phase 7/8 bumps, North Star item 9, etc.). They're sitting on your disk unstaged. You'll want to either commit them as a separate "docs: roadmap merge for Phase 6 Outposts" commit before T1, or fold them into T1's commit. The brief I'm about to give you for T1 will assume the roadmap is committed.

---

You said the path is T1 + T2 + T4. That's correct — that's the chain to working remote terminal:

- **T1**: bridge daemon (its own repo) — proves the wire works.
- **T2**: host-side `outpost:tailscale-host` Fitting — proves Garrison can talk to it.
- **T4**: terminal Fitting gains outpost-awareness — proves the user-facing payoff.

**T3 (bootstrap) deferrable.** For tonight you can manually SCP the bridge to the development machine, set the config, run it. The bootstrap script just packages those manual steps. Skipping T3 saves real time without losing function.

**Time budget remaining for tonight:** depends on what's left in your day. T1 + T2 + T4 is realistically 3-4 hours if T0 took 90 minutes and went smoothly. Tighter if anything bites.

Drafting T1 next. It's the largest of the three (a new repo with the bridge daemon implementing every operation namespace). I'll keep it scoped to the minimum that lets T2 talk to it — process + fs + git + exec — and explicitly defer any nice-to-haves that don't block T2/T4.

T1 brief staged. Notes worth reading before pasting:

**1. Three flagged items in §13 are real, in order of likelihood to bite:**

- **Process vs PTY in `process.spawn`** is the most likely surprise. T4 (terminal Fitting) genuinely needs PTY to render terminals correctly. If T0's spec didn't settle this, T1 will hit it and need to either add a `tty: true` flag to the protocol or commit to PTY-by-default. Worth letting Claude Code read the spec and flag if it's ambiguous.

- **Node version on remote machines.** If your development machine has Node 18 or older, T1 won't run there without an upgrade. Worth checking before T1 ships; if needed, the manual deployment (§11) gains a "use nvm to install Node 20" step.

- **WebSocket URL scheme.** `ws://` over Tailscale is fine — Tailscale provides the transport encryption layer. `wss://` would add no security but require certificate management nonsense. The spec should be definitive; if it isn't, default to `ws://`.

**2. The launchd plist with placeholder substitution is intentional.** It ships as a template; the user (or T3 later) fills in `__BRIDGE_PATH__` and `__HOME__` at install time. Avoids hardcoding paths that vary per machine.

**3. Mock host as the smoke-test target.** §10 specifies that T1 doesn't need a working T2 to be done — it just needs a tiny test WebSocket server in `examples/` that the bridge can talk to. This decouples T1 and T2 cleanly; Claude Code can ship T1 without waiting on T2. Then T2 implements the real host side and you connect them.

**4. Manual deployment is explicit in §11.** This is the tonight-only path that lets you skip T3. After T1 is in GitHub, you SSH from automation to development, clone, build, configure, run. Five commands, takes 5 minutes. T3 will automate this later.

T2 brief comes next — host-side Fitting. I can draft it now in parallel (you'll need it to make T1's bridge actually useful), or wait until you've started T1. Your call.

The PTY routing logic itself doesn't change — xterm.js doesn't know if the bytes it receives come from a local PTY or a remote one over the bridge. The change is in *where* the PTY spawn request goes.

T4 brief staged. Three things worth knowing before pasting:

**1. The PTY question is the only real risk.** §12 flags it as the first item, and §3.3 walks through what to do if T0's spec didn't settle it. This is the most likely place T4 hits a wall — if PTY isn't first-class in `process.spawn`, Claude Code should surface that and propose a fix to the spec rather than work around it. Working around a missing PTY would mean a broken terminal experience (no proper resize, weird wrap behavior, lost control sequences).

**2. The "local" entry in the dropdown is a synthetic UI concept, not an outpost.** §12 item 4 calls this out. Don't be tempted to model local as a special outpost — that complicates everything and gains nothing. The dropdown UI just adds "local" as a static first entry, and the spawn handler branches on selection.

**3. The manual smoke in §13 is your tonight checklist.** Eight steps from "open Workbench" to "doing Ekoa work via the bridge." If T4 ships and those eight steps pass, your tonight goal is met. If something breaks at step 4 or 5, that's the bug to fix immediately rather than continuing with the rest of T4's spec.

**On the broader trajectory:** after T4 you have the minimal viable Phase 6. T5 (worktree-management on outpost) and T6 (session-view on outpost) are quality-of-life — you can `git worktree add` manually in an SSH'd terminal tonight, but the Garrison UI version would be nicer this week. T7 (Operative-side outpost-actions) and T8 (vault-sync) are the broader bridge payoff. None of those block tonight.

Standing by for "T4 done" — and ideally a screenshot of `hostname` on the development machine appearing in a terminal inside automation machine's Garrison UI. That's the moment Phase 6 becomes real.

### assistant — 2026-05-11 14:41 UTC

 Smart framing — and you've spotted a real gap. Phase 6 wires the *transport* across machines but not the *learning*. The bridge can spawn a session on the development machine; it doesn't have an answer for "what does that session learn, where does the learning go, and how does the rest of the Operative benefit."

Before I sketch what this becomes, let me reflect back what I think I heard so we don't talk past each other:

---

**The problem, restated:**

The Operative's "knowledge" today is the union of several stores, mostly host-machine-local:

1. **Compiled memory** — `~/Projects/ekus/obsidian-vault/Compiled/` on the automation machine. Generated by SessionEnd hooks from your Claude Code sessions.
2. **Project-level CLAUDE.md and skill prompts** — live with each project's repo, machine-specific.
3. **Credentials and tokens** — per-machine; you log into things on the machine you actually use them on.
4. **Trello / Calendar / Slack state** — currently host-machine only (the Fittings run there).
5. **Skill prompts and Fitting `for_consumers` text** — versioned in the Garrison repo, same across machines if Garrison is in sync.

When you work on the development machine through the bridge, several of these get out of sync or are simply absent:

- A Claude Code session spawned via `process.spawn` on the development machine runs as that machine's user. Its SessionEnd hook (if installed) writes to development's local memory-compiler output, not automation's vault. Memory diverges.
- Project work happens with credentials that live on development (your actual coding logins). The Operative on automation can't see them.
- Skills the agent learns on the development machine (refining a workflow, discovering a pattern) stay there.

The architecture wants a *single* knowledge surface for the Operative regardless of which machine produced the learning. The bridge gave us hands; we need to give us a shared brain.

**The second concept you raised: self-improving skills.**

Hermes and similar agents apparently treat skills as *evolving* artifacts — the agent runs tasks, observes outcomes, and updates the skill's prompt/instructions/tools accordingly. Today Garrison's skills are mostly static (the `for_consumers` block ships with the Fitting, gets updated by humans). A self-improving discipline would mean: the agent generates updates to skill prompts based on what it learns from real runs, those updates land in the skill (with some review gate), and future agents benefit.

The connection to multi-machine: if a skill is going to self-improve, the learning needs a *single* place to land, not N machine-local copies. The bridge is the transport; what we need is a clear answer to "where does the learning live, and who curates it."

---

**Three things I want to push on before sketching a phase:**

**1. Are these one phase or two?**

I see them as connected but separable:

- **Memory consolidation across machines** — the multi-machine knowledge surface. Vault sync (Phase 6 T8) is a partial answer. Real answer is more thorough: SessionEnd hooks on outposts push back to the host-canonical store; Trello/Calendar/Slack stay host-only and outposts read through; credentials live where they live and the Operative reads them through the bridge where needed.

- **Self-improving skills** — the learning loop. Skills evolve based on outcomes. This works in single-machine too; multi-machine just changes "where does the new version of the skill land."

The first is foundational for the second (you can't have skills improving across machines if memory isn't unified across machines first), but the first is also valuable standalone.

**My lean: one phase, two halves.** Memory consolidation is the foundation; self-improving skills is the payoff. Doing them in one phase keeps the design coherent — the same "this is where learning lives" answer serves both.

**2. What's the existing prior art you want me to research?**

You mentioned Hermes. There's also:

- **Anthropic's own work on Skills** (the Skills framework that you've been using for SKILL.md files — there are research signals about how this is evolving).
- **Voyager / generative-skill agents** in the games/embodied-AI space — agents that write their own skills.
- **CAMEL, AutoGPT, BabyAGI, Devin** — varying approaches to learning loops, most of them shallow.
- **Anthropic's Constitutional AI / RLAIF** — the broader research direction Anthropic is taking. Probably most directly relevant.

I should web-search a few of these to ground the phase plan in current state-of-the-art rather than what I happen to remember.

**3. The "review gate" question is the architecturally important one.**

Self-improving skills sound great until the agent writes itself a worse skill. Two failure modes:

- **Drift** — the skill slowly mutates away from useful behavior as the agent over-fits to recent runs.
- **Sabotage** — adversarial input convinces the agent to update a skill in a way that compromises future runs.

Three review-gate models exist:

- **Human review** — the agent proposes; you approve. Slow but safe.
- **Agent self-review** — a second agent (or the same agent in a meta-mode) reviews the proposed update. Fast but inherits the same blind spots.
- **Empirical review** — proposed updates run in a sandbox against a benchmark of past tasks; only updates that improve performance land. Hardest to build but most rigorous.

Phase X needs to pick a model (or specify how to combine them).

---

**Sketch of what this becomes:**

I'd call it **Phase 9 — Knowledge & Self-Improvement** (after Tasks Faculty). It bundles:

1. **Memory consolidation across outposts.** SessionEnd hooks on remote machines push to a host-canonical store. Credentials proxy. Vault as the single source of truth. The bridge is the transport.
2. **Skills evolution mechanism.** Skills can produce proposed updates based on outcomes. A review gate determines what lands.
3. **Outcome capture.** Tasks (Phase 8) provide the ground truth — did the work succeed? Did the user have to redo it? This is the signal skills evolve against.
4. **Multi-machine cohesion.** The Operative on the host orchestrates outposts; learning from any outpost benefits the next session on any outpost.

This phase **depends on Phase 8 (Tasks)** for outcome signals — you can't tell if a skill is improving without a way to mark "this task succeeded / this task got redone." So the natural order is: 6 → 7 → 8 → 9.

Three open questions for the actual phase plan when we get there:

1. **What's the single-source-of-truth for the canonical memory store?** Vault on automation machine? A new "knowledge base" Faculty?
2. **What's the skill-update review gate model?** (Human/agent/empirical, or hybrid.)
3. **What's the granularity of "outcome"?** Task completion (Phase 8 already has this), or finer-grained (per-tool-call, per-step)?

---

**What I want to do now:**

Park this in the roadmap as a placeholder Phase 9, with the key questions noted, but **not detailed**. The detailed planning waits until you're closer to Phase 8 — at that point we'll have Phase 6 (memory transport via bridge) and Phase 8 (outcomes via Tasks) both real, which is the context needed for Phase 9 to be well-specified.

I'll also add the research question (skill self-improvement prior art) to the parking lot so I remember to do the web-search work when we get there. Doing it now would be premature — Phases 6/7/8 may shift the right answer.

Want me to:

Roadmap updated. Three additions:

**1. Phase 9 placeholder** between Tasks (Phase 8) and the Cross-cutting UI surfaces section. The placeholder records:
- The "why" (two connected concerns the existing phases don't address).
- Dependencies on Phase 6 (transport) and Phase 8 (outcomes).
- The six open questions a detailed plan will need to settle.
- An explicit "what's NOT in this placeholder" section so we don't mistake it for a worked spec.

**2. Parking-lot entry** for the prior-art research (Hermes, Voyager, Anthropic's Skills evolution, RLAIF, plus the "what not to do" agents). Marked as a precondition for Phase 9 detailed planning — premature now.

**3. Decision log entry** dated today recording the placeholder addition, with the same reasoning compressed.

The placeholder format does two important things:

- **Keeps the gap visible** — every time you scan the roadmap, you see Phase 9 sitting there with its open questions. The credential-sync issue, the SessionEnd-hook-on-outposts issue, the where-does-canonical-knowledge-live issue — they don't get lost.
- **Refuses to over-specify** — when we get to detailed planning (probably during Phase 7 or 8), we'll have real signal from how memory and outcomes actually flow. Designing Phase 9 in detail now would lock in assumptions we can't validate yet.

Back to T4. Standing by for either "T4 done" or "T4 needs a tweak" — whichever comes first.

### assistant — 2026-05-11 15:23 UTC

 Good framing. Let me reflect back what I think you're describing, then push on it.

---

**The use case for Phase 9, restated:**

Four machines (automation, development, portable, office), one Operative, work happens wherever you sit. Today you switch between machines with Jump Desktop because Garrison only exists on one. After Phases 6-9, you sit at any machine, work locally, and Garrison absorbs whatever was learned there.

Two distinct learning paths matter:

1. **In-Garrison work** — terminals, sub-agents, automations, Operative chats. Garrison can instrument these because it controls the entry points.
2. **Outside-Garrison work** — you open Claude Code directly on the development machine and work on Indie or some other project. Garrison doesn't see it natively. The question is whether to *capture it via configuration* (the memory-compiler hooks installed on every Mac that does Claude Code work) or *sync after the fact* (a nightly job that walks the machines).

Your lean reads as configuration — install the hooks on every machine, capture per-session as it happens, sync the compiled output to the canonical store via the bridge. I agree; the alternative (nightly sync of raw logs) tries to reconstruct what the hooks already capture cleanly.

---

**Your three categories — memory, skills, automations.**

These are right, with one architectural correction worth making:

- **Memory** = the compiled understanding the Operative carries forward. Includes user preferences, project context, decisions, patterns. Captured by SessionEnd hooks on every Claude Code session.
- **Skills** (skill-agents) = the capabilities the Operative invokes. `coding-subagent`, `outpost-actions`, `projects-index`, etc. Today these are static; Phase 9 makes them evolve.
- **Automations** = Playwright flows (Phase 7) and similar. Your stated discipline: feedback-driven improvement with manual edit gates. Different shape from skills — more like recorded scripts that get refined than agents that get smarter.

The architectural correction: **skills and automations are two flavors of the same underlying pattern** — *evolvable artifacts that the Operative uses, that have a review discipline.* Phase 9's job is to define that pattern. Then skills and automations are specific applications of it.

That's clarifying because it tells us Phase 9 isn't really three workstreams (memory, skills, automations). It's two:

1. **Memory consolidation** — the *knowledge* layer.
2. **Evolvable artifacts** — the *capabilities* layer, of which skills and automations are concrete instances.

---

**What else belongs in Phase 9?**

You asked the right question. Let me list candidates and push back on each.

### Strong candidates

**1. `for_consumers` blocks.** Today these are static, edited by humans. They're effectively *prompts the Operative reads to decide when to use a Faculty/Fitting.* If skills can evolve, `for_consumers` can evolve too — same pattern. When the Operative discovers it should have used a Faculty but didn't, or used it wrong, the `for_consumers` block needs updating. This is the same architectural concern as skill evolution; should travel under the same review discipline.

**Verdict: yes, in Phase 9.**

**2. Orchestrator prompt + Soul declarations.** These are the agent's *identity* and *routing* prompts. They've been edited by humans throughout. If everything else self-improves, does the Soul too?

**Verdict: I'd push back on including this.** The Soul is the agent's stable self. Letting it self-edit is exactly the path to drift you flagged earlier. Phase 9 should explicitly *exclude* Soul/Orchestrator from self-improvement and document why. (Possible future phase could revisit, but not Phase 9.)

**3. Composition choices.** Which Fittings the Operative has in its composition is currently a human decision. Could the Operative learn "I should have had X Fitting installed"? Probably — but that's an "agent that designs its own Operative" capability, which is a different beast from "agent that improves its own skills." Out of scope.

**Verdict: out for Phase 9.**

### Candidates you didn't mention but should consider

**4. `for_consumers` decisions about *when not to use* something.** This is genuinely different from "improving how a skill works." It's "the Operative learns that for context X, skill Y is the wrong choice." This is what the Hermes-style discipline really cares about — negative learning. Easy to underweight.

**Verdict: belongs in Phase 9, probably under the `for_consumers` evolution work.**

**5. Tier classification.** Phase 1's Classifier assigns tiers; Phase 4's quick-task escape uses them. Today the tier rubric is static. Should it evolve? When the Operative misclassifies (rated tier-3 something the user wanted done immediately, or vice versa) — does that feed back into the tier rubric?

**Verdict: yes, but as a sub-area of "outcome capture." The Classifier itself doesn't self-improve; the tier rubric that informs the Classifier evolves.**

**6. Project context (CLAUDE.md files in each project).** These are local to each repo. Across machines they're version-controlled, so git handles sync. But the *learning into them* — when the Operative discovers a project's deploy command or its weird-quirk-with-X — should that land in the project's CLAUDE.md? Or in the global compiled memory?

**Verdict: in Phase 9, but as a question to settle, not a confident answer yet. Probably "both — the global memory points at the project-local CLAUDE.md as authoritative for project specifics."**

**7. Skill *invocation patterns*.** Distinct from skill *content*. The Operative learns "I almost always call projects-index → describe_project → read_project_file in that order; should that be a single composed call?" This is *learning workflows* on top of skills, not learning individual skills.

**Verdict: borderline. Mention as a future direction; not Phase 9 scope.**

### What I'd cut from your list

Nothing major. Your three (memory, skills, automations) are right at the high level. The expansion I'm proposing keeps memory as-is and splits the rest into "evolvable artifacts" with concrete instances being skills, automations, and `for_consumers` blocks.

---

**A structural concern worth raising now:**

You mentioned Karpathy's research and four layers of memory but said the ChatGPT pass was bad. Worth doing it properly here — but **only at the point where we plan Phase 9 in detail.** Doing the research now and shelving it for months risks the research getting stale; doing it just before Phase 9 detailed planning makes it actionable.

The placeholder I added to the roadmap already says this: research is parked, detailed planning waits until Phase 8 is in flight. I'd keep it that way.

---

**Two questions for you:**

_[tool_use: `ask_user_input_v0`]_

```json
{"questions": [{"options": ["Yes — update Phase 9 placeholder with the memory/evolvable-artifacts framing", "Not yet — keep the placeholder as-is, refine when we plan in detail", "Update some parts but not all — let's discuss"], "question": "Should I update the Phase 9 placeholder now with this clearer framing?"}, {"options": ["Yes — Operative auto-captures via hooks on each machine, plus bridge sync", "No — simpler nightly walker that reads each machine's Claude Code logs", "Hybrid — hooks on Garrison-managed sessions, walker for direct-Claude-Code sessions"], "question": "On the configure-vs-sync question: lean configure (memory-compiler hooks on every Mac)?"}]}
```

_[tool_result: `ask_user_input_v0`]_

Roadmap updated. Three changes:

**1. Phase 9 placeholder rewritten** with the two-pillar framing:
- Pillar A (Knowledge layer) — single canonical memory store, configure-not-walker discipline, SessionEnd hooks on every Mac including direct-Claude-Code sessions.
- Pillar B (Evolvable artifacts layer) — skills, automations, `for_consumers` blocks as three flavors of one underlying pattern.
- Explicit "what's OUT" section: Soul/Orchestrator self-improvement, composition self-design, real-time skill evolution, skill invocation pattern learning. These are deliberate exclusions.
- Open questions expanded to 8 from 6 (added direct-Claude-Code session capture discipline, negative learning shape).

**2. Decision log entry refreshed** to record the two-pillar framing decision dated today, plus note the office machine being added to the canonical list.

**3. Cross-cutting context updated** — four Macs now in the canonical machine list, with the goal ("stop using Jump Desktop") explicit. Office machine added with a "details when relevant" placeholder since you mentioned it lightly.

A few things worth noting:

**1. The "configure, not walker" decision is a real commitment.** It means every Mac that runs Claude Code needs the memory-compiler hooks installed. If you add a fifth machine someday, that's a step. If you reformat a machine, that's a step. The setup script can detect missing hooks and prompt, but the discipline is "install on every machine that does work." Tracked as open question #7 in the placeholder.

**2. Pillar B's unification means Phase 7 (Automations) and Phase 9 will interact.** Phase 7 will ship automations with its own feedback-and-improvement loop. Phase 9 will then retrofit automations into the shared evolvable-artifact pattern — meaning some Phase 7 work gets touched during Phase 9. The phase plan acknowledges this as Phase 7 → 9 retrofit work.

**3. The "negative learning" shape (open question #8) is the architecturally interesting one.** "Don't use skill Y in context X" is genuinely different from "improve skill Y." It might want its own representation (an anti-pattern registry) or it might just be `for_consumers` updates. Worth real research time when we plan in detail.

The placeholder is now coherent enough to reason about without locking in answers. When you eventually plan Phase 9 in detail — likely while Phase 8 is in flight — this is the starting point.

Back to T4. Standing by for the Claude Code summary when you've made the worktree fixes you mentioned.

T5 is in: machine selector, project selector, per-machine memory, prefs store, both local and outpost paths working through one route. That's solid.

What's left to close out Phase 6:

- **T6** — `session-view:sequoias` consuming worktree list (now multi-target) + terminal sessions, aggregate status across outposts, action wiring (open PR, kill, refocus)
- **T7** — `outpost-actions` agent-skill Fitting so the Operative can `run_on(machine, cmd)`, `read_file_on(...)`, etc.

Three things worth knowing before I draft this:

**1. Doing T6–T9 in one sitting is more aggressive than T0–T5.** Each is small individually, but they exercise different surfaces (session aggregation UI, agent-skill scaffolding, scheduled file sync, doc-writing). Reasonable estimate: 3–5 hours if everything goes cleanly. Worth saying out loud before you paste.

**2. T8 (vault-sync) has a real architectural decision T0's protocol probably didn't pre-settle.** The bridge has `fs.read`, `fs.write`, `fs.list`, `fs.watch`. None of those is "rsync this directory." vault-sync has to *implement* sync on top of those primitives — walk source, walk target, compute diff, write changed files. That's a real chunk of code, not a thin wrapper. Mentioning so the brief sets expectation correctly.

**3. T9 (verification) wants to be honest about state.** Some Phase 6 done-when items the user can only verify after using it for a day or two (vault sync staying current, bridge reconnect after sleep/wake). The verification doc should mark these as "runtime pending" the way prior phases did.

A few things worth flagging before you paste:

On reading the T5 transcript: Claude Code did right by you.** When you said "this is supposed to work across projects on any machine," Claude Code investigated, found T5 in the execution plan was literally about that, and shipped it cleanly. ### assistant — 2026-05-11 16:44 UTC

 Right — let me lay out what I added to the placeholder explicitly, then push on what's still genuinely open.

---

**What I added to the Phase 9 placeholder beyond the obvious three (memory, skills, automations):**

The clarifying move was reframing your three as **two pillars**:

- **Pillar A** is just memory (the knowledge layer).
- **Pillar B** is one pattern — *evolvable artifacts* — with three concrete instances: skills, automations, and `for_consumers` blocks.

That `for_consumers` addition is the one I want to make sure you saw. Today these blocks are static prompts that tell the Operative *when to use* a Faculty. They're functionally identical to skill content from an evolution standpoint — they get consulted, used or not used, and the outcome reveals whether the guidance was good. If skills can self-improve, these have to as well, or you get the weird state where the *content* of a skill evolves but the *guidance for when to invoke it* stays stale.

I also added:

- **Tier rubric evolution.** Phase 1's Classifier assigns tiers; the rubric it uses is static. When the Classifier misjudges (rated a quick task as tier-3, or vice versa), the rubric should evolve. The Classifier itself doesn't self-improve; the rubric it consults does.

And I **explicitly excluded:**

- **Soul + Orchestrator prompts.** These are the agent's stable self and routing logic. Letting them self-edit is the drift path.
- **Composition self-design.** "Which Fittings should this Operative have" stays human-decided. That's "agent designs its own Operative" territory, a different beast.
- **Real-time evolution.** Updates land on a slower cadence with review gates. No "improves itself mid-session."
- **Skill invocation patterns.** Workflow learning ("I always call A → B → C — make this one composed call?"). Future direction, not Phase 9.

That's what's there now.

---

**On the hooks-at-user-level decision:**

Good — that's the simpler discipline. Install `~/.claude/settings.json` hooks once per machine; every Claude Code session on that machine flows through them regardless of project. Same memory-compiler hooks you already use today. Setup script for each new machine becomes "make sure the hooks are installed; if not, install them now and prompt the user to verify."

The implication for the roadmap is that your "configure, not walker" discipline (the question you answered earlier) is even simpler than I framed it. Not "install hooks on every project" — just "install hooks once per machine."

I should note this in the Phase 9 placeholder.

---

**Now: what else should Phase 9 address?**

Five candidates, in rough order of importance:

**1. The Operative's *identity* on remote machines.**

When you spawn a Claude Code session on the development machine via the bridge, *whose session is it*? Today it's a plain Claude Code session running as your user on that machine, with no Operative identity wrapped around it. Memory it generates gets compiled, but the resulting compiled output is just "stuff Goncalo did" — not "stuff Verity (the Operative) helped with."

This matters for self-improvement because the outcome signal (Phase 8 Tasks) and the work that produced it (Phase 4 coding-subagent + Phase 6 outpost-actions) need to be linkable. If a skill update gets proposed based on outcomes, it has to know *which Operative's skill* and *which session's outcomes*.

Possible answers:

- **The Operative wraps remote sessions.** When the user opens a terminal on development via Garrison, the spawn includes an Operative-flavored system prompt addition. The session becomes an "Operative-extended" session.
- **The session is plain Claude Code; the memory-compiler tags compiled output with metadata** ("this output came from a session you opened from Garrison's Workbench, target=development, at timestamp X").
- **Both** — Operative-extended for Garrison-spawned sessions, tagged-only for sessions the user opens directly.

I'd lean both. The user runs plain Claude Code outside Garrison sometimes (you said this) and we don't want to force-wrap those. But Garrison-spawned sessions can carry the Operative context.

**This belongs in Phase 9.**

**2. Provenance and conflict resolution.**

If a skill is updated on Monday based on outcomes from the development machine and on Tuesday based on outcomes from the office machine, and both updates touch the same skill, what happens?

Three possible answers:

- **Linear timestamped updates** — last write wins per skill section. Simple; risks losing learning.
- **Three-way merge** — like git, with the canonical store as the merge base. Hard but principled.
- **Update *proposals* not direct updates** — every learning event proposes a change; a periodic review pass batches and reconciles proposals. Slowest but cleanest.

I'd lean proposals + periodic review. The review can be human at first, automated later. Aligns naturally with the review-gate question already in the placeholder.

**This belongs in Phase 9.**

**3. "How much memory" — the truncation / forgetting question.**

The compiled memory grows. Today the Obsidian vault has years of compiled material, most of it irrelevant to any given session. The Operative reads the *index* and looks up specific articles as needed — that's the current pattern, and it works.

But with multi-machine usage, the volume goes up significantly. Sessions on four machines, all writing daily. Do we need:

- **Tiered memory** (hot/warm/cold)?
- **Periodic compaction** (older memories distilled into summaries)?
- **Topic-based partitioning** (the Operative reads only memory relevant to current project/context)?

I'd lean "this is a real concern for Phase 9 but the right answer depends on what the volumes actually look like." Note as an open question; revisit when we have real multi-machine data.

**Maybe Phase 9, maybe later. Worth noting now so we don't forget.**

**4. The "private vs shared" memory question.**

Some memory is personal (your preferences, your communication style). Some is project-specific (Ekoa architectural decisions). Some is cross-project (your patterns as a developer).

Today the Operative reads the whole compiled vault. With self-improving skills, the question gets sharper: when a skill update is proposed based on Ekoa work, does it update the *global* version of the skill or an *Ekoa-scoped* version?

This connects to project context (`CLAUDE.md` files) which the placeholder already mentions. Worth being explicit: Phase 9 has to settle the scoping model. Probably: skills exist globally; project-scoped overrides via project-local skill files (like `.garrison/skills/` in each repo).

**This belongs in Phase 9.**

**5. The "agent reads its own memory of being wrong" loop.**

The most powerful self-improvement signal is *the agent reflecting on its own past mistakes*. Hermes and similar systems do this: after a task, generate a "lessons learned" entry that the agent reads on its next similar task. This is memory feeding *back into the agent's working context*, not just into a corpus the agent searches.

The Operative today reads the compiled memory at session start (Phase 1 SessionStart hook). It doesn't have a "and here are the lessons learned from your three most recent similar tasks" surface.

This is the *learning loop closure* — without it, the agent gathers experience but doesn't actively consult it. The compiled memory is reference material; the lessons-learned layer is feedback.

**This belongs in Phase 9, and I'd argue it's the most important addition.**

---

**Summary of what I'd add to the placeholder:**

The two-pillar framing stays. Within the pillars, expand:

- **Pillar A (knowledge):** add memory scoping (global vs project), volume management as an open question, hooks-at-user-level discipline confirmed.
- **Pillar B (evolvable artifacts):** add provenance/conflict resolution, update-proposals-with-review pattern.
- **New: Operative identity across machines.** The wrapping question.
- **New: Lessons-learned feedback loop.** The agent actively consults recent outcomes.

Want me to update the placeholder with all five? Or are some of these worth pushing back on first?

Phase 9 placeholder updated. Five additions landed:

**1. Hooks-at-user-level confirmed.** Pillar A's capture-discipline section now says "one canonical Claude Code settings (`~/.claude/settings.json`) reused across all machines. Hooks install there once per machine; every Claude Code session flows through them regardless of project." The decision is documented; future detailed planning starts from this answer rather than relitigating it.

**2. Memory scoping (Pillar A).** Global skills + project-local overrides via `.garrison/skills/<name>.md` per repo. Phase 2's project-index Fitting becomes the natural detector for "we're in project X, pull in those overrides." Lean answer with the open question "how do overrides compose — replace or merge?" parked for detailed planning.

**3. Provenance / conflict resolution (Pillar B).** Update-proposal model, not direct writes. Every learning event proposes a change; periodic review batches and reconciles. Same review machinery as the existing review-gate question. Eliminates the "last write wins" loss-of-learning failure mode.

**4. New Pillar C — Operative identity across machines.** Two paths: Garrison-spawned sessions get Operative-wrapped (system prompt material, taggable metadata); direct Claude Code sessions get tagged-only (Operative can see they happened, treats as user-driven). This is what makes outcome → artifact-update attribution actually work in a multi-machine world.

**5. New Pillar D — Lessons-learned feedback loop.** The "what I learned" generation tied to Phase 8 task completion, retrieval at SessionStart (naive keyword matching for v1), pruning policy for old lessons. This is the pillar with the most direct user-visible payoff: "the agent actually got better at this kind of task over time."

Also added: a decision log entry today recording the expansion, and the "What's IN Phase 9" list grew from 6 items to 10, open questions from 8 to 14.

The placeholder now reads as a *coherent four-pillar architecture* rather than a "memory + skills" sketch. When detailed planning happens (probably during Phase 8), this is the starting point. The prior-art research item in the parking lot still applies — Hermes/Voyager/Karpathy/RLAIF research informs the review-gate question (#3) and the lessons-learned mechanism (Pillar D) most directly.

Back to Phase 6 completion. Standing by for the implementation summary when that ships.
