### assistant — 2026-05-14 08:30 UTC

Yeah, you're laying out the real complexity here. Using the parent PID approach is actually elegant — if Garrison spawns a process, it can monitor that process tree directly via Unix tools like `lsof` or `ps` to see open ports, file handles, child processes, all of it. No need for the spawned processes to report back; Garrison just observes them. That sidesteps the whole "did the process forget to tell us it stopped" problem.

But that assumes Garrison is the direct parent, which might not always be true if sessions spawn things indirectly. > 
> I'm thinking about the brief structure now — starting with context about why Garrison needs holistic visibility, then the scope of what Monitor tracks, the integration approach via optional consumption, and flagging the open questions for implementation. spawned processes (sessions + subprocesses + servers)
> - Each spawned entity has: PID, status, logs, ports, network, env, cwd, uptime, parent/children
> - Source of truth: parent PID observation via Unix tools (lsof, ps, /proc)
> - Each Fitting that needs a UI serves its own UI on its own port (canonical pattern locked)
> - Monitor exposes its own React + esbuild UI on a known port
> - Web channel updated to optionally consume Monitor → button appears if available, opens Monitor UI
> 
> ## Out of scope
> - File browser integration (later faculty)
> - Voice
> - Modifying spawned processes (kill, signal, etc. — maybe later)
> - Multi-host monitoring
> 
> ## Architecture
> 
> ### Faculty role
> - Tracks all entities spawned by Garr
> 
> The Monitor walks Garrison's process tree to discover all descendants, pulling PID, command, status, start time, parent/child relationships, open ports via lsof, network connections, and working directory from standard Unix tools. It tails stdout/stderr where possible, though this works cleanly only if Garrison itself launches the processes, and detects when processes terminate to update their status.
> 
> The key question is what we can actually observe through the process tree versus what requires the spawned process to actively report back—particularly for logs, since tailing a child's output only works if Garrison captured those streams at spawn time, and nested spawns might not be accessible. I need to investigate whether we can always capture this or if we need a sidecar reporting protocol for deeper process hierarchies.
> 
> The Monitor UI runs as a standalone Node service on a conventional port, serving a React app built with esbuild following the same pattern as the web channel, and each Fitting that needs its own UI follows that same model.
> 
> The interface itself is responsive with cards on desktop and stacked mobile layouts—each card shows the process status, name, PID, uptime, open ports as clickable links, and basic stats like CPU and memory. Drilling into a card reveals the full command line, working directory, environment variables, all ports and network connections, plus the process tree showing parent and child relationships.
> 
> For the log viewer, I'm building a scrollable interface that follows new output in real-time, with toggles between stdout and stderr, timestamps on each line, and search/filter capability added later if it's straightforward. The process tree itself will be a collapsible visualization of the parent-child hierarchy.
> 
> On the architectural side, each UI-bearing Fitting serves its own interface on its own port, and other Fittings reference it by URL rather than importing components directly—this becomes the canonical pattern for Garrison UI Fittings that I'll document. Web channel integration is next.
> 
> The Monitor Faculty becomes optional consumption—at runtime the web channel checks if a Monitor Fitting is available via a health check against its expected port, and if it exists, shows a "Monitor" button that opens the UI in a new tab or panel. If not available, the button stays hidden. I'm still deciding whether to do a one-time availability check on load or poll periodically.
> 
> For implementation, I'm starting with a feasibility audit: surveying what's observable about processes on Linux and macOS through tools like `ps`, `lsof`, `/proc`, and `netstat`, then figuring out how Garrison can wrap process spawning to capture stdout and stderr to a log. Now I'm implementing the Monitor Fitting itself—building a process tree walker that periodically polls for updates and pushes them via SSE, then registering it with Garrison and verifying it can track spawned processes with live status changes. After that comes the UI layer: a React-based dashboard with a card grid, drill-down details, log viewer, and process tree visualization that connects via SSE for real-time updates, all mobile-responsive and verified to display active processes and their logs correctly.
> 
> Then I need to wire the Monitor into the web channel by adding an optional dependency check, showing a button only when Monitor is available, and handling the UI differently for desktop versus mobile—opening in a new tab on desktop or as an overlay panel on mobile. Finally, I'll document the key architectural decisions in DECISIONS.md. Faculty list
> - Process tree / parent PID observation is the primary discovery mechanism
> - Each UI-bearing Fitting serves its own UI on its own port (canonical pattern)
> - Cross-Fitting UI integration is via URL link to known port, not component sharing
> - Optional capability consumption pattern used by web channel for Monitor
> 
> ## Documents to create/update
> - New section in CAPABILITIES.md for `monitor` capability kind
> - New UI-FITTINGS.md (or section in existing) for the per-Fitting-own-UI-on-own-port pattern
> - DECISIONS.md entries for the above
>

> 4. Optional consumption availability check: lightweight ping, registry query, or both?
> 5. Web channel UI: open Monitor as new tab/window, in-app panel, or both depending on viewport?

Brief's ready. A few things worth flagging:

1. **I locked in three architectural decisions** in the brief, not just for the Monitor: (a) each UI-bearing Fitting serves its own UI on its own port, (b) cross-Fitting UI integration is via URL link only — no component sharing or state passing, (c) the optional-consumption pattern (web channel checks availability and degrades gracefully). These apply retroactively to the web channel too.

2. **Monitor expands beyond the v1 five-kind vocabulary** — both as a new Faculty and a new capability kind. The brief flags this explicitly since v1 had locked at five kinds. Given the "no release, whatever's useful" scope shift, fine, but worth recording in `DECISIONS.md`.

3. **Phase 1 is explicitly a feasibility audit** — what's observable cross-platform, and especially the log capture question for nested spawns. The brief tells Claude Code not to start the backend until this lands.

4. **Read-only for now** — no killing/signaling processes from the Monitor. We can add that later if you want it; saying so up front keeps the brief honest.
